data.title: Identify and name common plants
data.subject: Science
data.description: identify and name a variety of common wild and garden plants, including deciduous and evergreen trees
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should use the local environment throughout the year to explore and answer questions about plants growing in their habitat. Where possible, they should observe the growth of flowers and vegetables that they have planted. They should become familiar with common names of flowers, examples of deciduous and evergreen trees, and plant structures (including leaves, flowers (blossom), petals, fruit, roots, bulb, seed, trunk, branches, stem).
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: observing closely, perhaps using magnifying glasses, and comparing and contrasting familiar plants; describing how they were able to identify and group them, and drawing diagrams showing the parts of different plants including trees. Pupils might keep records of how plants have changed over time, for example, the leaves falling off trees and buds opening; and compare and contrast what they have found out about different plants.

data.title: Identify and describe plant structure
data.subject: Science
data.description: identify and describe the basic structure of a variety of common flowering plants, including trees
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should use the local environment throughout the year to explore and answer questions about plants growing in their habitat. Where possible, they should observe the growth of flowers and vegetables that they have planted. They should become familiar with common names of flowers, examples of deciduous and evergreen trees, and plant structures (including leaves, flowers (blossom), petals, fruit, roots, bulb, seed, trunk, branches, stem).
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: observing closely, perhaps using magnifying glasses, and comparing and contrasting familiar plants; describing how they were able to identify and group them, and drawing diagrams showing the parts of different plants including trees. Pupils might keep records of how plants have changed over time, for example, the leaves falling off trees and buds opening; and compare and contrast what they have found out about different plants.

data.title: Observe seasonal changes
data.subject: Science
data.description: observe changes across the 4 seasons
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should observe and talk about changes in the weather and the seasons. Note: pupils should be warned that it is not safe to look directly at the sun, even when wearing dark glasses.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: making tables and charts about the weather; and making displays of what happens in the world around them, including day length, as the seasons change.

data.title: Observe and describe seasonal weather and day length
data.subject: Science
data.description: observe and describe weather associated with the seasons and how day length varies
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should observe and talk about changes in the weather and the seasons. Note: pupils should be warned that it is not safe to look directly at the sun, even when wearing dark glasses.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: making tables and charts about the weather; and making displays of what happens in the world around them, including day length, as the seasons change.

data.title: Offspring growing into adults
data.subject: Science
data.description: notice that animals, including humans, have offspring which grow into adults
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should be introduced to the basic needs of animals for survival, as well as the importance of exercise and nutrition for humans. They should also be introduced to the processes of reproduction and growth in animals. The focus at this stage should be on questions that help pupils to recognise growth; they should not be expected to understand how reproduction occurs.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: observing, through video or first-hand observation and measurement, how different animals, including humans, grow; asking questions about what things animals need for survival and what humans need to stay healthy; and suggesting ways to find answers to their questions.

data.title: Basic needs for survival
data.subject: Science
data.description: find out about and describe the basic needs of animals, including humans, for survival (water, food and air)
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should be introduced to the basic needs of animals for survival, as well as the importance of exercise and nutrition for humans.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: observing, through video or first-hand observation and measurement, how different animals, including humans, grow; asking questions about what things animals need for survival and what humans need to stay healthy.

data.title: Exercise, nutrition, and hygiene
data.subject: Science
data.description: describe the importance for humans of exercise, eating the right amounts of different types of food, and hygiene
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should be introduced to the basic needs of animals for survival, as well as the importance of exercise and nutrition for humans.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: observing, through video or first-hand observation and measurement, how different animals, including humans, grow; asking questions about what things animals need for survival and what humans need to stay healthy; and suggesting ways to find answers to their questions.

data.title: Living, dead, and never alive
data.subject: Science
data.description: explore and compare the differences between things that are living, dead, and things that have never been alive
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should be introduced to the idea that all living things have certain characteristics that are essential for keeping them alive and healthy. They should raise and answer questions that help them to become familiar with the life processes that are common to all living things. Pupils should be introduced to the terms 'habitat' and 'microhabitat'. They should raise and answer questions about the local environment that help them to identify and study a variety of plants and animals within their habitat and observe how living things depend on each other. Pupils should compare animals in familiar habitats with animals found in less familiar habitats, for example, on the seashore, in woodland, in the ocean, in the rainforest.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: sorting and classifying things according to whether they are living, dead or were never alive, and recording their findings using charts. They could construct a simple food chain that includes humans (eg, grass, cow, human). They could describe the conditions in different habitats and microhabitats and find out how the conditions affect the number and types of plants and animals that live there.

data.title: Habitats and basic needs
data.subject: Science
data.description: identify that most living things live in habitats to which they are suited and describe how different habitats provide for the basic needs of different kinds of animals and plants, and how they depend on each other
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should be introduced to the idea that all living things have certain characteristics that are essential for keeping them alive and healthy. They should raise and answer questions that help them to become familiar with the life processes that are common to all living things. Pupils should be introduced to the terms 'habitat' and 'microhabitat'.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: sorting and classifying things according to whether they are living, dead or were never alive, and recording their findings using charts. They could construct a simple food chain that includes humans (eg, grass, cow, human).

data.title: Plants and animals in habitats
data.subject: Science
data.description: identify and name a variety of plants and animals in their habitats, including microhabitats
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should be introduced to the terms 'habitat' and 'microhabitat'. They should raise and answer questions about the local environment that help them to identify and study a variety of plants and animals within their habitat and observe how living things depend on each other.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: sorting and classifying things according to whether they are living, dead or were never alive, and recording their findings using charts.

data.title: Simple food chains
data.subject: Science
data.description: describe how animals obtain their food from plants and other animals, using the idea of a simple food chain, and identify and name different sources of food
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should be introduced to the idea that all living things have certain characteristics that are essential for keeping them alive and healthy.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: sorting and classifying things according to whether they are living, dead or were never alive, and recording their findings using charts. They could construct a simple food chain that includes humans (eg, grass, cow, human).

data.title: Seeds and bulbs growing
data.subject: Science
data.description: observe and describe how seeds and bulbs grow into mature plants
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should use the local environment throughout the year to observe how plants grow. Pupils should be introduced to the requirements of plants for germination, growth and survival, as well as the processes of reproduction and growth in plants. Note: seeds and bulbs need water to grow but most do not need light; seeds and bulbs have a store of food inside them.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: observing and recording, with some accuracy, the growth of a variety of plants as they change over time from a seed or bulb, or observing similar plants at different stages of growth; setting up a comparative test to show that plants need light and water to stay healthy.

data.title: Plant needs for growth
data.subject: Science
data.description: find out and describe how plants need water, light and a suitable temperature to grow and stay healthy
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should use the local environment throughout the year to observe how plants grow. Pupils should be introduced to the requirements of plants for germination, growth and survival.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: observing and recording, with some accuracy, the growth of a variety of plants as they change over time from a seed or bulb; setting up a comparative test to show that plants need light and water to stay healthy.

data.title: Suitability of everyday materials
data.subject: Science
data.description: identify and compare the suitability of a variety of everyday materials, including wood, metal, plastic, glass, brick, rock, paper and cardboard for particular uses
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should identify and discuss the uses of different everyday materials so that they become familiar with how some materials are used for more than one thing or different materials are used for the same thing. They should think about the properties of materials that make them suitable or unsuitable for particular purposes and they should be encouraged to think about unusual and creative uses for everyday materials. Pupils might find out about people who have developed useful new materials, for example John Dunlop, Charles Macintosh or John McAdam.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: comparing the uses of everyday materials in and around the school with materials found in other places; observing closely, identifying and classifying the uses of different materials, and recording their observations.

data.title: Changing shapes of solid objects
data.subject: Science
data.description: find out how the shapes of solid objects made from some materials can be changed by squashing, bending, twisting and stretching
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should identify and discuss the uses of different everyday materials so that they become familiar with how some materials are used for more than one thing or different materials are used for the same thing.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: comparing the uses of everyday materials in and around the school with materials found in other places; observing closely, identifying and classifying the uses of different materials, and recording their observations.

data.title: Animal and human nutrition
data.subject: Science
data.description: identify that animals, including humans, need the right types and amount of nutrition, and that they cannot make their own food; they get nutrition from what they eat
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should continue to learn about the importance of nutrition and should be introduced to the main body parts associated with the skeleton and muscles, finding out how different parts of the body have special functions.
data.workingScientifically: They might compare and contrast the diets of different animals and decide ways of grouping them according to what they eat. They might research different food groups and how they keep us healthy, and design meals based on what they find out.

data.title: Skeletons and muscles
data.subject: Science
data.description: identify that humans and some other animals have skeletons and muscles for support, protection and movement
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should continue to learn about the importance of nutrition and should be introduced to the main body parts associated with the skeleton and muscles, finding out how different parts of the body have special functions.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: identifying and grouping animals with and without skeletons and observing and comparing their movement; exploring ideas about what would happen if humans did not have skeletons.

data.title: Movement on different surfaces
data.subject: Science
data.description: compare how things move on different surfaces
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should observe that magnetic forces can act without direct contact, unlike most forces, where direct contact is necessary (for example, opening a door, pushing a swing).
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: comparing how different things move and grouping them; raising questions and carrying out tests to find out how far things move on different surfaces, and gathering and recording data to find answers to their questions.

data.title: Contact and non-contact forces
data.subject: Science
data.description: notice that some forces need contact between 2 objects, but magnetic forces can act at a distance
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should observe that magnetic forces can act without direct contact, unlike most forces, where direct contact is necessary (for example, opening a door, pushing a swing).
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: comparing how different things move and grouping them.

data.title: Magnetic attraction and repulsion
data.subject: Science
data.description: observe how magnets attract or repel each other and attract some materials and not others
data.notesAndGuidance: They should explore the behaviour and everyday uses of different magnets (for example, bar, ring, button and horseshoe).
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: exploring the strengths of different magnets and finding a fair way to compare them; sorting materials into those that are magnetic and those that are not.

data.title: Grouping magnetic materials
data.subject: Science
data.description: compare and group together a variety of everyday materials on the basis of whether they are attracted to a magnet, and identify some magnetic materials
data.notesAndGuidance: They should explore the behaviour and everyday uses of different magnets (for example, bar, ring, button and horseshoe).
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: sorting materials into those that are magnetic and those that are not.

data.title: Magnetic poles
data.subject: Science
data.description: describe magnets as having 2 poles
data.notesAndGuidance: They should explore the behaviour and everyday uses of different magnets (for example, bar, ring, button and horseshoe).
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: looking for patterns in the way that magnets behave in relation to each other and what might affect this, for example, the strength of the magnet or which pole faces another.

data.title: Predicting magnetic interactions
data.subject: Science
data.description: predict whether 2 magnets will attract or repel each other, depending on which poles are facing
data.notesAndGuidance: They should explore the behaviour and everyday uses of different magnets (for example, bar, ring, button and horseshoe).
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: looking for patterns in the way that magnets behave in relation to each other and what might affect this, for example, the strength of the magnet or which pole faces another; identifying how these properties make magnets useful in everyday items and suggesting creative uses for different magnets.

data.title: Light for seeing and dark as absence of light
data.subject: Science
data.description: recognise that they need light in order to see things and that dark is the absence of light
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should explore what happens when light reflects off a mirror or other reflective surfaces. They should think about why it is important to protect their eyes from bright lights. Note: pupils should be warned that it is not safe to look directly at the sun, even when wearing dark glasses.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: looking for patterns in what happens to shadows when the light source moves or the distance between the light source and the object changes.

data.title: Reflection of light
data.subject: Science
data.description: notice that light is reflected from surfaces
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should explore what happens when light reflects off a mirror or other reflective surfaces, including playing mirror games to help them to answer questions about how light behaves.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: looking for patterns in what happens to shadows when the light source moves or the distance between the light source and the object changes.

data.title: Dangers of sunlight and eye protection
data.subject: Science
data.description: recognise that light from the sun can be dangerous and that there are ways to protect their eyes
data.notesAndGuidance: They should think about why it is important to protect their eyes from bright lights. Note: pupils should be warned that it is not safe to look directly at the sun, even when wearing dark glasses.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: looking for patterns in what happens to shadows when the light source moves or the distance between the light source and the object changes.

data.title: Formation of shadows
data.subject: Science
data.description: recognise that shadows are formed when the light from a light source is blocked by an opaque object
data.notesAndGuidance: They should look for, and measure, shadows, and find out how they are formed and what might cause the shadows to change.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: looking for patterns in what happens to shadows when the light source moves or the distance between the light source and the object changes.

data.title: Patterns in shadow size
data.subject: Science
data.description: find patterns in the way that the size of shadows change
data.notesAndGuidance: They should look for, and measure, shadows, and find out how they are formed and what might cause the shadows to change.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: looking for patterns in what happens to shadows when the light source moves or the distance between the light source and the object changes.

data.title: Functions of plant parts
data.subject: Science
data.description: identify and describe the functions of different parts of flowering plants: roots, stem/trunk, leaves and flowers
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should be introduced to the relationship between structure and function: the idea that every part has a job to do. They should explore questions that focus on the role of the roots and stem in nutrition and support, leaves for nutrition and flowers for reproduction.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: comparing the effect of different factors on plant growth, for example, the amount of light, the amount of fertiliser; discovering how seeds are formed by observing the different stages of plant life cycles over a period of time.

data.title: Requirements for plant life and growth
data.subject: Science
data.description: explore the requirements of plants for life and growth (air, light, water, nutrients from soil, and room to grow) and how they vary from plant to plant
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should be introduced to the relationship between structure and function: the idea that every part has a job to do.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: comparing the effect of different factors on plant growth, for example, the amount of light, the amount of fertiliser.

data.title: Water transport in plants
data.subject: Science
data.description: investigate the way in which water is transported within plants
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should explore questions that focus on the role of the roots and stem in nutrition and support, leaves for nutrition and flowers for reproduction.
data.workingScientifically: They might observe how water is transported in plants, for example, by putting cut, white carnations into coloured water and observing how water travels up the stem to the flowers.

data.title: Role of flowers in the plant life cycle
data.subject: Science
data.description: explore the part that flowers play in the life cycle of flowering plants, including pollination, seed formation and seed dispersal
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should explore questions that focus on the role of the roots and stem in nutrition and support, leaves for nutrition and flowers for reproduction.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: discovering how seeds are formed by observing the different stages of plant life cycles over a period of time; looking for patterns in the structure of fruits that relate to how the seeds are dispersed.

data.title: Comparing and grouping rocks
data.subject: Science
data.description: compare and group together different kinds of rocks on the basis of their appearance and simple physical properties
data.notesAndGuidance: Linked with work in geography, pupils should explore different kinds of rocks and soils, including those in the local environment.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: observing rocks, including those used in buildings and gravestones, and exploring how and why they might have changed over time; using a hand lens or microscope to help them to identify and classify rocks according to whether they have grains or crystals, and whether they have fossils in them.

data.title: Fossil formation
data.subject: Science
data.description: describe in simple terms how fossils are formed when things that have lived are trapped within rock
data.notesAndGuidance: Linked with work in geography, pupils should explore different kinds of rocks and soils, including those in the local environment.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might research and discuss the different kinds of living things whose fossils are found in sedimentary rock and explore how fossils are formed.

data.title: Composition of soils
data.subject: Science
data.description: recognise that soils are made from rocks and organic matter
data.notesAndGuidance: Linked with work in geography, pupils should explore different kinds of rocks and soils, including those in the local environment.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils could explore different soils and identify similarities and differences between them and investigate what happens when rocks are rubbed together or what changes occur when they are in water. They can raise and answer questions about the way soils are formed.

data.title: Functions of the digestive system
data.subject: Science
data.description: describe the simple functions of the basic parts of the digestive system in humans
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should be introduced to the main body parts associated with the digestive system, for example: mouth, tongue, teeth, oesophagus, stomach, and small and large intestine, and explore questions that help them to understand their special functions.
data.workingScientifically: They might draw and discuss their ideas about the digestive system and compare them with models or images.

data.title: Types and functions of teeth
data.subject: Science
data.description: identify the different types of teeth in humans and their simple functions
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should be introduced to the main body parts associated with the digestive system, for example: mouth, tongue, teeth, oesophagus, stomach, and small and large intestine.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: comparing the teeth of carnivores and herbivores and suggesting reasons for differences; finding out what damages teeth and how to look after them.

data.title: Food chains, producers, predators and prey
data.subject: Science
data.description: construct and interpret a variety of food chains, identifying producers, predators and prey
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should be introduced to the main body parts associated with the digestive system and explore questions that help them to understand their special functions.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: comparing the teeth of carnivores and herbivores and suggesting reasons for differences.

data.title: Appliances that run on electricity
data.subject: Science
data.description: identify common appliances that run on electricity
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should construct simple series circuits, trying different components, for example, bulbs, buzzers and motors, and including switches, and use their circuits to create simple devices.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: observing patterns, for example, that bulbs get brighter if more cells are added, that metals tend to be conductors of electricity.

data.title: Constructing simple series circuits
data.subject: Science
data.description: construct a simple series electrical circuit, identifying and naming its basic parts, including cells, wires, bulbs, switches and buzzers
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should construct simple series circuits, trying different components, for example, bulbs, buzzers and motors, and including switches, and use their circuits to create simple devices. Pupils should draw the circuit as a pictorial representation, not necessarily using conventional circuit symbols at this stage.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: observing patterns, for example, that bulbs get brighter if more cells are added.

data.title: Identifying complete loops in circuits
data.subject: Science
data.description: identify whether or not a lamp will light in a simple series circuit, based on whether or not the lamp is part of a complete loop with a battery
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should construct simple series circuits, trying different components, for example, bulbs, buzzers and motors, and including switches, and use their circuits to create simple devices.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: observing patterns, for example, that bulbs get brighter if more cells are added.

data.title: Function of switches in circuits
data.subject: Science
data.description: recognise that a switch opens and closes a circuit and associate this with whether or not a lamp lights in a simple series circuit
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should construct simple series circuits, trying different components, for example, bulbs, buzzers and motors, and including switches, and use their circuits to create simple devices.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: observing patterns, for example, that some materials can and some cannot be used to connect across a gap in a circuit.

data.title: Conductors and insulators
data.subject: Science
data.description: recognise some common conductors and insulators, and associate metals with being good conductors
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should construct simple series circuits, trying different components. Note: pupils might use the terms current and voltage, but these should not be introduced or defined formally at this stage.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: observing patterns, for example, that metals tend to be conductors of electricity, and that some materials can and some cannot be used to connect across a gap in a circuit.

data.title: Grouping living things
data.subject: Science
data.description: recognise that living things can be grouped in a variety of ways
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should use the local environment throughout the year to raise and answer questions that help them to identify and study plants and animals in their habitat. They should identify how the habitat changes throughout the year. Pupils should explore possible ways of grouping a wide selection of living things that include animals, flowering plants and non-flowering plants.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: using and making simple guides or keys to explore and identify local plants and animals; making a guide to local living things.

data.title: Using classification keys
data.subject: Science
data.description: explore and use classification keys to help group, identify and name a variety of living things in their local and wider environment
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should use the local environment throughout the year to raise and answer questions that help them to identify and study plants and animals in their habitat.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: using and making simple guides or keys to explore and identify local plants and animals; making a guide to local living things; raising and answering questions based on their observations of animals.

data.title: Environmental changes and dangers
data.subject: Science
data.description: recognise that environments can change and that this can sometimes pose dangers to living things
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should use the local environment throughout the year to raise and answer questions that help them to identify and study plants and animals in their habitat. They should identify how the habitat changes throughout the year.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: raising and answering questions based on their observations of animals and what they have found out about other animals that they have researched.

data.title: Grouping solids, liquids and gases
data.subject: Science
data.description: compare and group materials together, according to whether they are solids, liquids or gases
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should explore a variety of everyday materials and develop simple descriptions of the states of matter (solids hold their shape; liquids form a pool not a pile; gases escape from an unsealed container). Pupils should observe water as a solid, a liquid and a gas.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: grouping and classifying a variety of different materials; exploring the effect of temperature on substances such as chocolate, butter, cream.

data.title: Changes of state from heating and cooling
data.subject: Science
data.description: observe that some materials change state when they are heated or cooled, and measure or research the temperature at which this happens in degrees Celsius
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should observe water as a solid, a liquid and a gas and should note the changes to water when it is heated or cooled. Note: teachers should avoid using materials where heating is associated with chemical change, for example, through baking or burning.
data.workingScientifically: They could research the temperature at which materials change state.

data.title: Evaporation, condensation and the water cycle
data.subject: Science
data.description: identify the part played by evaporation and condensation in the water cycle and associate the rate of evaporation with temperature
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should observe water as a solid, a liquid and a gas and should note the changes to water when it is heated or cooled.
data.workingScientifically: They might observe and record evaporation over a period of time, and investigate the effect of temperature on washing drying or snowmen melting.

data.title: How sounds are made (vibrations)
data.subject: Science
data.description: identify how sounds are made, associating some of them with something vibrating
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should explore and identify the way sound is made through vibration in a range of different musical instruments from around the world; and find out how the pitch and volume of sounds can be changed in a variety of ways.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: finding patterns in the sounds that are made by different objects such as saucepan lids of different sizes or elastic bands of different thicknesses.

data.title: Sound vibrations travelling to the ear
data.subject: Science
data.description: recognise that vibrations from sounds travel through a medium to the ear
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should explore and identify the way sound is made through vibration in a range of different musical instruments from around the world.
data.workingScientifically: They might make earmuffs from a variety of different materials to investigate which provides the best insulation against sound.

data.title: Patterns between pitch and object features
data.subject: Science
data.description: find patterns between the pitch of a sound and features of the object that produced it
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should explore and identify the way sound is made through vibration in a range of different musical instruments from around the world; and find out how the pitch and volume of sounds can be changed in a variety of ways.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: finding patterns in the sounds that are made by different objects such as saucepan lids of different sizes or elastic bands of different thicknesses.

data.title: Patterns between volume and vibration strength
data.subject: Science
data.description: find patterns between the volume of a sound and the strength of the vibrations that produced it
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should find out how the pitch and volume of sounds can be changed in a variety of ways.
data.workingScientifically: They could make and play their own instruments by using what they have found out about pitch and volume.

data.title: Sound fading over distance
data.subject: Science
data.description: recognise that sounds get fainter as the distance from the sound source increases
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should explore and identify the way sound is made through vibration in a range of different musical instruments from around the world.
data.workingScientifically: They could make and play their own instruments by using what they have found out about pitch and volume.

data.title: Human development to old age
data.subject: Science
data.description: describe the changes as humans develop to old age
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should draw a timeline to indicate stages in the growth and development of humans. They should learn about the changes experienced in puberty.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils could work scientifically by researching the gestation periods of other animals and comparing them with humans; by finding out and recording the length and mass of a baby as it grows.

data.title: Movement of Earth and planets
data.subject: Science
data.description: describe the movement of the Earth and other planets relative to the sun in the solar system
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should be introduced to a model of the sun and Earth that enables them to explain day and night. Pupils should learn that the sun is a star at the centre of our solar system and that it has 8 planets.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: comparing the time of day at different places on the Earth; creating simple models of the solar system.

data.title: Movement of the moon
data.subject: Science
data.description: describe the movement of the moon relative to the Earth
data.notesAndGuidance: They should understand that a moon is a celestial body that orbits a planet.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: creating simple models of the solar system.

data.title: Spherical bodies in space
data.subject: Science
data.description: describe the sun, Earth and moon as approximately spherical bodies
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should learn that the sun is a star at the centre of our solar system and that it has 8 planets. They should understand that a moon is a celestial body that orbits a planet.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: creating simple models of the solar system.

data.title: Earth's rotation, day and night
data.subject: Science
data.description: use the idea of the Earth's rotation to explain day and night and the apparent movement of the sun across the sky
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should be introduced to a model of the sun and Earth that enables them to explain day and night. Pupils should find out about the way that ideas about the solar system have developed, understanding how the geocentric model gave way to the heliocentric model by considering the work of scientists such as Ptolemy, Alhazen and Copernicus.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: comparing the time of day at different places on the Earth; constructing simple shadow clocks and sundials.

data.title: Gravity and falling objects
data.subject: Science
data.description: explain that unsupported objects fall towards the Earth because of the force of gravity acting between the Earth and the falling object
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should explore falling objects and raise questions about the effects of air resistance. They should explore the effects of air resistance by observing how different objects such as parachutes and sycamore seeds fall.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: exploring falling paper cones or cupcake cases, and designing and making a variety of parachutes and carrying out fair tests to determine which designs are the most effective.

data.title: Air resistance, water resistance and friction
data.subject: Science
data.description: identify the effects of air resistance, water resistance and friction, that act between moving surfaces
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should experience forces that make things begin to move, get faster or slow down. Pupils should explore the effects of friction on movement and find out how it slows or stops moving objects.
data.workingScientifically: They might explore resistance in water by making and testing boats of different shapes.

data.title: Mechanisms and force effects
data.subject: Science
data.description: recognise that some mechanisms including levers, pulleys and gears allow a smaller force to have a greater effect
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should explore the effects of levers, pulleys and simple machines on movement.
data.workingScientifically: They might design and make products that use levers, pulleys, gears and/or springs and explore their effects.

data.title: Differences in life cycles
data.subject: Science
data.description: describe the differences in the life cycles of a mammal, an amphibian, an insect and a bird
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should study and raise questions about their local environment throughout the year. They should observe life-cycle changes in a variety of living things, for example, plants in the vegetable garden or flower border, and animals in the local environment. They should find out about the work of naturalists and animal behaviourists, for example, David Attenborough and Jane Goodall.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: observing and comparing the life cycles of plants and animals in their local environment with other plants and animals around the world; asking pertinent questions and suggesting reasons for similarities and differences.

data.title: Reproduction in plants and animals
data.subject: Science
data.description: describe the life process of reproduction in some plants and animals
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should find out about different types of reproduction, including sexual and asexual reproduction in plants, and sexual reproduction in animals.
data.workingScientifically: They might try to grow new plants from different parts of the parent plant, for example, seeds, stem and root cuttings, tubers, bulbs. They might observe changes in an animal over a period of time, comparing how different animals reproduce and grow.

data.title: Compare and group everyday materials
data.subject: Science
data.description: compare and group together everyday materials on the basis of their properties, including their hardness, solubility, transparency, conductivity (electrical and thermal), and response to magnets
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should build a more systematic understanding of materials by exploring and comparing the properties of a broad range of materials, including relating these to what they learnt about magnetism in year 3 and about electricity in year 4.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: carrying out tests to answer questions, for example, 'Which materials would be the most effective for making a warm jacket, for wrapping ice cream to stop it melting, or for making blackout curtains?'

data.title: Dissolving and recovering substances
data.subject: Science
data.description: know that some materials will dissolve in liquid to form a solution, and describe how to recover a substance from a solution
data.notesAndGuidance: They should explore reversible changes, including evaporating, filtering, sieving, melting and dissolving, recognising that melting and dissolving are different processes.
data.workingScientifically: They might compare materials in order to make a switch in a circuit.

data.title: Separating mixtures
data.subject: Science
data.description: use knowledge of solids, liquids and gases to decide how mixtures might be separated, including through filtering, sieving and evaporating
data.notesAndGuidance: They should explore reversible changes, including evaporating, filtering, sieving, melting and dissolving, recognising that melting and dissolving are different processes.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: carrying out tests to answer questions.

data.title: Reasons for uses of everyday materials
data.subject: Science
data.description: give reasons, based on evidence from comparative and fair tests, for the particular uses of everyday materials, including metals, wood and plastic
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should build a more systematic understanding of materials by exploring and comparing the properties of a broad range of materials.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: carrying out tests to answer questions, for example, 'Which materials would be the most effective for making a warm jacket, for wrapping ice cream to stop it melting, or for making blackout curtains?'

data.title: Reversible changes
data.subject: Science
data.description: demonstrate that dissolving, mixing and changes of state are reversible changes
data.notesAndGuidance: They should explore reversible changes, including evaporating, filtering, sieving, melting and dissolving, recognising that melting and dissolving are different processes.
data.workingScientifically: They could observe and compare the changes that take place when burning different materials or baking bread or cakes.

data.title: Irreversible changes and new materials
data.subject: Science
data.description: explain that some changes result in the formation of new materials, and that this kind of change is not usually reversible, including changes associated with burning and the action of acid on bicarbonate of soda
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should explore changes that are difficult to reverse, for example, burning, rusting and other reactions, for example, vinegar with bicarbonate of soda.
data.workingScientifically: They could observe and compare the changes that take place when burning different materials or baking bread or cakes. They might research and discuss how chemical changes have an impact on our lives.

data.title: Human circulatory system
data.subject: Science
data.description: identify and name the main parts of the human circulatory system, and describe the functions of the heart, blood vessels and blood
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should build on their learning from years 3 and 4 about the main body parts and internal organs to explore and answer questions that help them to understand how the circulatory system enables the body to function.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: exploring the work of scientists and scientific research about the relationship between diet, exercise, drugs, lifestyle and health.

data.title: Impact of diet, exercise, drugs and lifestyle
data.subject: Science
data.description: recognise the impact of diet, exercise, drugs and lifestyle on the way their bodies function
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should learn how to keep their bodies healthy and how their bodies might be damaged - including how some drugs and other substances can be harmful to the human body.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: exploring the work of scientists and scientific research about the relationship between diet, exercise, drugs, lifestyle and health.

data.title: Transport of nutrients and water
data.subject: Science
data.description: describe the ways in which nutrients and water are transported within animals, including humans
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should build on their learning from years 3 and 4 about the main body parts and internal organs to explore and answer questions that help them to understand how the circulatory system enables the body to function.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: exploring the work of scientists and scientific research about the relationship between diet, exercise, drugs, lifestyle and health.

data.title: Brightness of a lamp or volume of a buzzer
data.subject: Science
data.description: associate the brightness of a lamp or the volume of a buzzer with the number and voltage of cells used in the circuit
data.notesAndGuidance: Building on their work in year 4, pupils should construct simple series circuits, to help them to answer questions about what happens when they try different components, for example, switches, bulbs, buzzers and motors.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: systematically identifying the effect of changing one component at a time in a circuit.

data.title: Variations in how components function
data.subject: Science
data.description: compare and give reasons for variations in how components function, including the brightness of bulbs, the loudness of buzzers and the on/off position of switches
data.notesAndGuidance: Building on their work in year 4, pupils should construct simple series circuits, to help them to answer questions about what happens when they try different components.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: systematically identifying the effect of changing one component at a time in a circuit; designing and making a set of traffic lights, a burglar alarm or some other useful circuit.

data.title: Recognised symbols for simple circuits
data.subject: Science
data.description: use recognised symbols when representing a simple circuit in a diagram
data.notesAndGuidance: They should learn how to represent a simple circuit in a diagram using recognised symbols. Note: pupils are expected to learn only about series circuits, not parallel circuits.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: designing and making a set of traffic lights, a burglar alarm or some other useful circuit.

data.title: Living things change over time and fossils
data.subject: Science
data.description: recognise that living things have changed over time and that fossils provide information about living things that inhabited the Earth millions of years ago
data.notesAndGuidance: Building on what they learned about fossils in the topic on rocks in year 3, pupils should find out more about how living things on earth have changed over time.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: observing and raising questions about local animals and how they are adapted to their environment; comparing how some living things are adapted to survive in extreme conditions.

data.title: Offspring vary and are not identical to parents
data.subject: Science
data.description: recognise that living things produce offspring of the same kind, but normally offspring vary and are not identical to their parents
data.notesAndGuidance: They should be introduced to the idea that characteristics are passed from parents to their offspring, for instance by considering different breeds of dogs.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: observing and raising questions about local animals and how they are adapted to their environment.

data.title: Adaptation and evolution
data.subject: Science
data.description: identify how animals and plants are adapted to suit their environment in different ways and that adaptation may lead to evolution
data.notesAndGuidance: They should also appreciate that variation in offspring over time can make animals more or less able to survive in particular environments.
data.workingScientifically: They might analyse the advantages and disadvantages of specific adaptations, such as being on 2 feet rather than 4, having a long or a short beak, having gills or lungs.

data.title: Light travels in straight lines
data.subject: Science
data.description: recognise that light appears to travel in straight lines
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should build on the work on light in year 3, exploring the way that light behaves, including light sources, reflection and shadows. They should talk about what happens and make predictions.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: deciding where to place rear-view mirrors on cars; designing and making a periscope and using the idea that light appears to travel in straight lines to explain how it works.

data.title: Objects are seen because they give out or reflect light
data.subject: Science
data.description: use the idea that light travels in straight lines to explain that objects are seen because they give out or reflect light into the eye
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should build on the work on light in year 3, exploring the way that light behaves, including light sources, reflection and shadows.
data.workingScientifically: They might investigate the relationship between light sources, objects and shadows by using shadow puppets.

data.title: How we see things
data.subject: Science
data.description: explain that we see things because light travels from light sources to our eyes or from light sources to objects and then to our eyes
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should build on the work on light in year 3, exploring the way that light behaves, including light sources, reflection and shadows.
data.workingScientifically: They might investigate the relationship between light sources, objects and shadows by using shadow puppets.

data.title: Shadows have the same shape as objects
data.subject: Science
data.description: use the idea that light travels in straight lines to explain why shadows have the same shape as the objects that cast them
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should build on the work on light in year 3, exploring the way that light behaves, including light sources, reflection and shadows.
data.workingScientifically: They might investigate the relationship between light sources, objects and shadows by using shadow puppets.

data.title: Classify living things into broad groups
data.subject: Science
data.description: describe how living things are classified into broad groups according to common observable characteristics and based on similarities and differences, including micro-organisms, plants and animals
data.notesAndGuidance: Pupils should build on their learning about grouping living things in year 4 by looking at the classification system in more detail. They should be introduced to the idea that broad groupings, such as micro-organisms, plants and animals can be subdivided. Through direct observations where possible, they should classify animals into commonly found invertebrates (such as insects, spiders, snails, worms) and vertebrates (fish, amphibians, reptiles, birds and mammals).
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: using classification systems and keys to identify some animals and plants in the immediate environment. They could research unfamiliar animals and plants from a broad range of other habitats and decide where they belong in the classification system.

data.title: Give reasons for classifying plants and animals
data.subject: Science
data.description: give reasons for classifying plants and animals based on specific characteristics
data.notesAndGuidance: They should discuss reasons why living things are placed in one group and not another.
data.workingScientifically: Pupils might work scientifically by: using classification systems and keys to identify some animals and plants in the immediate environment.
