data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Draw evidence from literary or informational texts to support analysis, reflection, and research.

data.title: W.8.9.a
data.cluster: Research to Build and Present Knowledge
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Apply grade 8 Reading standards to literature (e.g., “Analyze how a modern work of fiction draws on themes, patterns of events, or character types from myths, traditional stories, or religious works such as the Bible, including describing how the material is rendered new”).

data.title: W.8.9.b
data.cluster: Research to Build and Present Knowledge
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Apply grade 8 Reading standards to literary nonfiction (e.g., “Delineate and evaluate the argument and specific claims in a text, assessing whether the reasoning is sound and the evidence is relevant and sufficient; recognize when irrelevant evidence is introduced”).

data.title: W.9-10.1
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Write arguments to support claims in an analysis of substantive topics or texts, using valid reasoning and relevant and sufficient evidence.

data.title: W.9-10.10
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Write routinely over extended time frames (time for research, reflection, and revision) and shorter time frames (a single sitting or a day or two) for a range of tasks, purposes, and audiences.

data.title: W.9-10.1.a
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Introduce precise claim(s), distinguish the claim(s) from alternate or opposing claims, and create an organization that establishes clear relationships among claim(s), counterclaims, reasons, and evidence.

data.title: W.9-10.1.b
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Develop claim(s) and counterclaims fairly, supplying evidence for each while pointing out the strengths and limitations of both in a manner that anticipates the audience’s knowledge level and concerns.

data.title: W.9-10.1.c
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Use words, phrases, and clauses to link the major sections of the text, create cohesion, and clarify the relationships between claim(s) and reasons, between reasons and evidence, and between claim(s) and counterclaims.

data.title: W.9-10.1.d
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Establish and maintain a formal style and objective tone while attending to the norms and conventions of the discipline in which they are writing.

data.title: W.9-10.1.e
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Provide a concluding statement or section that follows from and supports the argument presented.

data.title: W.9-10.2
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Write informative/explanatory texts to examine and convey complex ideas, concepts, and information clearly and accurately through the effective selection, organization, and analysis of content.

data.title: W.9-10.2.a
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Introduce a topic; organize complex ideas, concepts, and information to make important connections and distinctions; include formatting (e.g., headings), graphics (e.g., figures, tables), and multimedia when useful to aiding comprehension.

data.title: W.9-10.2.b
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Develop the topic with well-chosen, relevant, and sufficient facts, extended definitions, concrete details, quotations, or other information and examples appropriate to the audience’s knowledge of the topic.

data.title: W.9-10.2.c
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Use appropriate and varied transitions to link the major sections of the text, create cohesion, and clarify the relationships among complex ideas and concepts.

data.title: W.9-10.2.d
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Use precise language and domain-specific vocabulary to manage the complexity of the topic.

data.title: W.9-10.2.e
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Establish and maintain a formal style and objective tone while attending to the norms and conventions of the discipline in which they are writing.

data.title: W.9-10.2.f
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Provide a concluding statement or section that follows from and supports the information or explanation presented (e.g., articulating implications or the significance of the topic).

data.title: W.9-10.3
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Write narratives to develop real or imagined experiences or events using effective technique, well-chosen details, and well-structured event sequences.

data.title: W.9-10.3.a
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Engage and orient the reader by setting out a problem, situation, or observation, establishing one or multiple point(s) of view, and introducing a narrator and/or characters; create a smooth progression of experiences or events.

data.title: W.9-10.3.b
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Use narrative techniques, such as dialogue, pacing, description, reflection, and multiple plot lines, to develop experiences, events, and/or characters.

data.title: W.9-10.3.c
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Use a variety of techniques to sequence events so that they build on one another to create a coherent whole.

data.title: W.9-10.3.d
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Use precise words and phrases, telling details, and sensory language to convey a vivid picture of the experiences, events, setting, and/or characters.

data.title: W.9-10.3.e
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Provide a conclusion that follows from and reflects on what is experienced, observed, or resolved over the course of the narrative.

data.title: W.9-10.4
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Produce clear and coherent writing in which the development, organization, and style are appropriate to task, purpose, and audience. (Grade-specific expectations for writing types are defined in standards 1–3 above.)

data.title: W.9-10.5
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Develop and strengthen writing as needed by planning, revising, editing, rewriting, or trying a new approach, focusing on addressing what is most significant for a specific purpose and audience. (Editing for conventions should demonstrate command of Language standards 1–3 up to and including grades 9–10 on page 55.)

data.title: W.9-10.6
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Use technology, including the Internet, to produce, publish, and update individual or shared writing products, taking advantage of technology’s capacity to link to other information and to display information flexibly and dynamically.

data.title: W.9-10.7
data.cluster: Research to Build and Present Knowledge
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Conduct short as well as more sustained research projects to answer a question (including a self-generated question) or solve a problem; narrow or broaden the inquiry when appropriate; synthesize multiple sources on the subject, demonstrating understanding of the subject under investigation.

data.title: W.9-10.8
data.cluster: Research to Build and Present Knowledge
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Gather relevant information from multiple authoritative print and digital sources, using advanced searches effectively; assess the usefulness of each source in answering the research question; integrate information into the text selectively to maintain the flow of ideas, avoiding plagiarism and following a standard format for citation.

data.title: W.9-10.9
data.cluster: Research to Build and Present Knowledge
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Draw evidence from literary or informational texts to support analysis, reflection, and research.

data.title: W.9-10.9.a
data.cluster: Research to Build and Present Knowledge
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Apply grades 9–10 Reading standards to literature (e.g., “Analyze how an author draws on and transforms source material in a specific work [e.g., how Shakespeare treats a theme or topic from Ovid or the Bible or how a later author draws on a play by Shakespeare]”).

data.title: W.9-10.9.b
data.cluster: Research to Build and Present Knowledge
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Apply grades 9–10 Reading standards to literary nonfiction (e.g., “Delineate and evaluate the argument and specific claims in a text, assessing whether the reasoning is valid and the evidence is relevant and sufficient; identify false statements and fallacious reasoning”).

data.title: WHST.11-12.1
data.subject: Literacy in History/Social Studies, Science, and Technical Subjects
data.description: Write arguments focused on discipline-specific content.

data.title: WHST.11-12.10
data.subject: Literacy in History/Social Studies, Science, and Technical Subjects
data.description: Write routinely over extended time frames (time for reflection and revision) and shorter time frames (a single sitting or a day or two) for a range of discipline-specific tasks, purposes, and audiences.

data.title: WHST.11-12.1a
data.subject: Literacy in History/Social Studies, Science, and Technical Subjects
data.description: Introduce precise, knowledgeable claim(s), establish the significance of the claim(s), distinguish the claim(s) from alternate or opposing claims, and create an organization that logically sequences the claim(s), counterclaims, reasons, and evidence.

data.title: WHST.11-12.1b
data.subject: Literacy in History/Social Studies, Science, and Technical Subjects
data.description: Develop claim(s) and counterclaims fairly and thoroughly, supplying the most relevant data and evidence for each while pointing out the strengths and limitations of both claim(s) and counterclaims in a discipline-appropriate form that anticipates the audience’s knowledge level, concerns, values, and possible biases.

data.title: WHST.11-12.1c
data.subject: Literacy in History/Social Studies, Science, and Technical Subjects
data.description: Use words, phrases, and clauses as well as varied syntax to link the major sections of the text, create cohesion, and clarify the relationships between claim(s) and reasons, between reasons and evidence, and between claim(s) and counterclaims.

data.title: WHST.11-12.1d
data.subject: Literacy in History/Social Studies, Science, and Technical Subjects
data.description: Establish and maintain a formal style and objective tone while attending to the norms and conventions of the discipline in which they are writing.

data.title: WHST.11-12.1e
data.subject: Literacy in History/Social Studies, Science, and Technical Subjects
data.description: Provide a concluding statement or section that follows from or supports the argument presented.

data.title: WHST.11-12.2
data.subject: Literacy in History/Social Studies, Science, and Technical Subjects
data.description: Write informative/explanatory texts, including the narration of historical events, scientific procedures/ experiments, or technical processes.

data.title: WHST.11-12.2a
data.subject: Literacy in History/Social Studies, Science, and Technical Subjects
data.description: Introduce a topic and organize complex ideas, concepts, and information so that each new element builds on that which precedes it to create a unified whole; include formatting (e.g., headings), graphics (e.g., figures, tables), and multimedia when useful to aiding comprehension.

data.title: WHST.11-12.2b
data.subject: Literacy in History/Social Studies, Science, and Technical Subjects
data.description: Develop the topic thoroughly by selecting the most significant and relevant facts, extended definitions, concrete details, quotations, or other information and examples appropriate to the audience’s knowledge of the topic.

data.title: WHST.11-12.2c
data.subject: Literacy in History/Social Studies, Science, and Technical Subjects
data.description: Use varied transitions and sentence structures to link the major sections of the text, create cohesion, and clarify the relationships among complex ideas and concepts.

data.title: WHST.11-12.2d
data.subject: Literacy in History/Social Studies, Science, and Technical Subjects
data.description: Use precise language, domain-specific vocabulary and techniques such as metaphor, simile, and analogy to manage the complexity of the topic; convey a knowledgeable stance in a style that responds to the discipline and context as well as to the expertise of likely readers.

data.subject: Literacy in History/Social Studies, Science, and Technical Subjects
data.description: Provide a concluding statement or section that follows from and supports the information or explanation provided (e.g., articulating implications or the significance of the topic).

data.title: W.K.1
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Use a combination of drawing, dictating, and writing to compose opinion pieces in which they tell a reader the topic or the name of the book they are writing about and state an opinion or preference about the topic or book (e.g., My favorite book is . . .).

data.title: W.K.2
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Use a combination of drawing, dictating, and writing to compose informative/explanatory texts in which they name what they are writing about and supply some information about the topic.

data.title: W.K.3
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Use a combination of drawing, dictating, and writing to narrate a single event or several loosely linked events, tell about the events in the order in which they occurred, and provide a reaction to what happened.

data.title: W.K.5
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: With guidance and support from adults, respond to questions and suggestions from peers and add details to strengthen writing as needed.

data.title: W.K.6
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: With guidance and support from adults, explore a variety of digital tools to produce and publish writing, including in collaboration with peers.

data.title: W.K.7
data.cluster: Research to Build and Present Knowledge
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: Participate in shared research and writing projects (e.g., explore a number of books by a favorite author and express opinions about them).

data.title: W.K.8
data.cluster: Research to Build and Present Knowledge
data.subject: English Language Arts
data.description: With guidance and support from adults, recall information from experiences or gather information from provided sources to answer a question.

curricula.name: Common Core State Standards for Mathematics
curricula.license: Verbatim standard text included under this source’s upstream license. See PROVENANCE.md.

data.title: Distinguish defining attributes of shapes
data.cluster: Reason with shapes and their attributes.
data.description: Distinguish between defining attributes (e.g., triangles are closed and three-sided) versus non-defining attributes (e.g., color, orientation, overall size); build and draw shapes to possess defining attributes.

data.title: Compose two-dimensional and three-dimensional shapes
data.cluster: Reason with shapes and their attributes.
data.description: Compose two-dimensional shapes (rectangles, squares, trapezoids, triangles, half-circles, and quarter-circles) or three-dimensional shapes (cubes, right rectangular prisms, right circular cones, and right circular cylinders) to create a composite shape, and compose new shapes from the composite shape.

data.title: Partition circles and rectangles
data.cluster: Reason with shapes and their attributes.
data.description: Partition circles and rectangles into two and four equal shares, describe the shares using the words halves, fourths, and quarters, and use the phrases half of, fourth of, and quarter of. Describe the whole as two of, or four of the shares. Understand for these examples that decomposing into more equal shares creates smaller shares.

data.title: Order and compare objects by length
data.cluster: Measure lengths indirectly and by iterating length units.
data.description: Order three objects by length; compare the lengths of two objects indirectly by using a third object.

data.title: Measure length with length units
data.cluster: Measure lengths indirectly and by iterating length units.
data.description: Express the length of an object as a whole number of length units, by laying multiple copies of a shorter object (the length unit) end to end; understand that the length measurement of an object is the number of same-size length units that span it with no gaps or overlaps. Limit to contexts where the object being measured is spanned by a whole number of length units with no gaps or overlaps.

data.title: Tell and write time
data.description: Tell and write time in hours and half-hours using analog and digital clocks.

data.title: Represent and interpret data
data.description: Organize, represent, and interpret data with up to three categories; ask and answer questions about the total number of data points, how many in each category, and how many more or less are in one category than in another.

data.title: Count to 120
data.description: Count to 120, starting at any number less than 120. In this range, read and write numerals and represent a number of objects with a written numeral.

data.title: Understand place value of two-digit numbers
data.description: Understand that the two digits of a two-digit number represent amounts of tens and ones. Understand the following as special cases:

data.title: 10 as a bundle of ten ones
data.description: 10 can be thought of as a bundle of ten ones — called a “ten.”

data.title: Composition of numbers 11 to 19
data.description: The numbers from 11 to 19 are composed of a ten and one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine ones.

data.title: Multiples of 10
data.description: The numbers 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90 refer to one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine tens (and 0 ones).

data.title: Compare two-digit numbers
data.description: Compare two two-digit numbers based on meanings of the tens and ones digits, recording the results of comparisons with the symbols >, =, and <.

data.title: Add within 100
data.cluster: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to add and subtract.
data.description: Add within 100, including adding a two-digit number and a one-digit number, and adding a two-digit number and a multiple of 10, using concrete models or drawings and strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction; relate the strategy to a written method and explain the reasoning used. Understand that in adding two-digit numbers, one adds tens and tens, ones and ones; and sometimes it is necessary to compose a ten.

data.title: Mentally find 10 more or 10 less
data.cluster: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to add and subtract.
data.description: Given a two-digit number, mentally find 10 more or 10 less than the number, without having to count; explain the reasoning used.

data.title: Subtract multiples of 10
data.cluster: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to add and subtract.
data.description: Subtract multiples of 10 in the range 10-90 from multiples of 10 in the range 10-90 (positive or zero differences), using concrete models or drawings and strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction; relate the strategy to a written method and explain the reasoning used.

data.title: Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction
data.cluster: Represent and solve problems involving addition and subtraction.
data.description: Use addition and subtraction within 20 to solve word problems involving situations of adding to, taking from, putting together, taking apart, and comparing, with unknowns in all positions, e.g., by using objects, drawings, and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.

data.title: Solve word problems with three whole numbers
data.cluster: Represent and solve problems involving addition and subtraction.
data.description: Solve word problems that call for addition of three whole numbers whose sum is less than or equal to 20, e.g., by using objects, drawings, and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.

data.title: Apply properties of operations
data.cluster: Understand and apply properties of operations and the relationship between addition and subtraction.
data.description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to add and subtract. Examples: If 8 + 3 = 11 is known, then 3 + 8 = 11 is also known. (Commutative property of addition.) To add 2 + 6 + 4, the second two numbers can be added to make a ten, so 2 + 6 + 4 = 2 + 10 = 12. (Associative property of addition.)

data.title: Subtraction as unknown-addend problem
data.cluster: Understand and apply properties of operations and the relationship between addition and subtraction.
data.description: Understand subtraction as an unknown-addend problem. For example, subtract 10 – 8 by finding the number that makes 10 when added to 8.

data.title: Relate counting to addition and subtraction
data.description: Relate counting to addition and subtraction (e.g., by counting on 2 to add 2).

data.title: Add and subtract within 20
data.description: Add and subtract within 20, demonstrating fluency for addition and subtraction within 10. Use strategies such as counting on; making ten (e.g., 8 + 6 = 8 + 2 + 4 = 10 + 4 = 14); decomposing a number leading to a ten (e.g., 13 – 4 = 13 – 3 – 1 = 10 – 1 = 9); using the relationship between addition and subtraction (e.g., knowing that 8 + 4 = 12, one knows 12 – 8 = 4); and creating equivalent but easier or known sums (e.g., adding 6 + 7 by creating the known equivalent 6 + 6 + 1 = 12 + 1 = 13).

data.title: Meaning of the equal sign
data.cluster: Work with addition and subtraction equations.
data.description: Understand the meaning of the equal sign, and determine if equations involving addition and subtraction are true or false. For example, which of the following equations are true and which are false? 6 = 6, 7 = 8 – 1, 5 + 2 = 2 + 5, 4 + 1 = 5 + 2.

data.title: Determine unknown whole number
data.cluster: Work with addition and subtraction equations.
data.description: Determine the unknown whole number in an addition or subtraction equation relating three whole numbers. For example, determine the unknown number that makes the equation true in each of the equations 8 + ? = 11, 5 = [] – 3, 6 + 6 = [].

data.title: Recognize and draw shapes
data.cluster: Reason with shapes and their attributes.
data.description: Recognize and draw shapes having specified attributes, such as a given number of angles or a given number of equal faces. Identify triangles, quadrilaterals, pentagons, hexagons, and cubes.

data.title: Partition a rectangle
data.cluster: Reason with shapes and their attributes.
data.description: Partition a rectangle into rows and columns of same-size squares and count to find the total number of them.

data.title: Partition circles and rectangles into shares
data.cluster: Reason with shapes and their attributes.
data.description: Partition circles and rectangles into two, three, or four equal shares, describe the shares using the words halves, thirds, half of, a third of, etc., and describe the whole as two halves, three thirds, four fourths. Recognize that equal shares of identical wholes need not have the same shape.

data.title: Measure length with appropriate tools
data.cluster: Measure and estimate lengths in standard units.
data.description: Measure the length of an object by selecting and using appropriate tools such as rulers, yardsticks, meter sticks, and measuring tapes.

data.title: Draw picture and bar graphs
data.description: Draw a picture graph and a bar graph (with single-unit scale) to represent a data set with up to four categories. Solve simple put-together, take-apart, and compare problems using information presented in a bar graph.

data.title: Measure length with different units
data.cluster: Measure and estimate lengths in standard units.
data.description: Measure the length of an object twice, using length units of different lengths for the two measurements; describe how the two measurements relate to the size of the unit chosen.

data.title: Estimate lengths
data.cluster: Measure and estimate lengths in standard units.
data.description: Estimate lengths using units of inches, feet, centimeters, and meters.

data.title: Compare lengths of objects
data.cluster: Measure and estimate lengths in standard units.
data.description: Measure to determine how much longer one object is than another, expressing the length difference in terms of a standard length unit.

data.title: Solve word problems involving length
data.cluster: Relate addition and subtraction to length.
data.description: Use addition and subtraction within 100 to solve word problems involving lengths that are given in the same units, e.g., by using drawings (such as drawings of rulers) and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.

data.title: Represent lengths on a number line
data.cluster: Relate addition and subtraction to length.
data.description: Represent whole numbers as lengths from 0 on a number line diagram with equally spaced points corresponding to the numbers 0, 1, 2, ..., and represent whole-number sums and differences within 100 on a number line diagram.

data.title: Tell and write time
data.description: Tell and write time from analog and digital clocks to the nearest five minutes, using a.m. and p.m.

data.title: Solve word problems with money
data.description: Solve word problems involving dollar bills, quarters, dimes, nickels, and pennies, using $ and ¢ symbols appropriately. Example: If you have 2 dimes and 3 pennies, how many cents do you have?

data.title: Generate measurement data and line plots
data.description: Generate measurement data by measuring lengths of several objects to the nearest whole unit, or by making repeated measurements of the same object. Show the measurements by making a line plot, where the horizontal scale is marked off in whole-number units.

data.title: Understand three-digit place value
data.domain: Number and Operations in Base Ten
data.description: Understand that the three digits of a three-digit number represent amounts of hundreds, tens, and ones; e.g., 706 equals 7 hundreds, 0 tens, and 6 ones. Understand the following as special cases:

data.title: 100 as a bundle of ten tens
data.domain: Number and Operations in Base Ten
data.description: 100 can be thought of as a bundle of ten tens — called a “hundred.”

data.title: Multiples of 100
data.domain: Number and Operations in Base Ten
data.description: The numbers 100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600, 700, 800, 900 refer to one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine hundreds (and 0 tens and 0 ones).

data.title: Count within 1000
data.domain: Number and Operations in Base Ten
data.description: Count within 1000; skip-count by 5s, 10s, and 100s.

data.title: Read and write numbers to 1000
data.domain: Number and Operations in Base Ten
data.description: Read and write numbers to 1000 using base-ten numerals, number names, and expanded form.

data.title: Compare three-digit numbers
data.domain: Number and Operations in Base Ten
data.description: Compare two three-digit numbers based on meanings of the hundreds, tens, and ones digits, using >, =, and < symbols to record the results of comparisons.

data.title: Fluently add and subtract within 100
data.domain: Number and Operations in Base Ten
data.cluster: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to add and subtract.
data.description: Fluently add and subtract within 100 using strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction.

data.title: Add up to four two-digit numbers
data.domain: Number and Operations in Base Ten
data.cluster: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to add and subtract.
data.description: Add up to four two-digit numbers using strategies based on place value and properties of operations.

data.title: Add and subtract within 1000
data.domain: Number and Operations in Base Ten
data.cluster: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to add and subtract.
data.description: Add and subtract within 1000, using concrete models or drawings and strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction; relate the strategy to a written method. Understand that in adding or subtracting three-digit numbers, one adds or subtracts hundreds and hundreds, tens and tens, ones and ones; and sometimes it is necessary to compose or decompose tens or hundreds.

data.title: Mentally add or subtract 10 or 100
data.domain: Number and Operations in Base Ten
data.cluster: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to add and subtract.
data.description: Mentally add 10 or 100 to a given number 100–900, and mentally subtract 10 or 100 from a given number 100–900.

data.title: Explain addition and subtraction strategies
data.domain: Number and Operations in Base Ten
data.cluster: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to add and subtract.
data.description: Explain why addition and subtraction strategies work, using place value and the properties of operations.

data.title: Use addition and subtraction within 100 to solve word problems
data.cluster: Represent and solve problems involving addition and subtraction.
data.description: Use addition and subtraction within 100 to solve one- and two-step word problems involving situations of adding to, taking from, putting together, taking apart, and comparing, with unknowns in all positions, e.g., by using drawings and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.

data.title: Fluently add and subtract within 20
data.description: Fluently add and subtract within 20 using mental strategies. By end of Grade 2, know from memory all sums of two one-digit numbers.

data.title: Determine odd or even numbers
data.cluster: Work with equal groups of objects to gain foundations for multiplication.
data.description: Determine whether a group of objects (up to 20) has an odd or even number of members, e.g., by pairing objects or counting them by 2s; write an equation to express an even number as a sum of two equal addends.

data.title: Use addition for rectangular arrays
data.cluster: Work with equal groups of objects to gain foundations for multiplication.
data.description: Use addition to find the total number of objects arranged in rectangular arrays with up to 5 rows and up to 5 columns; write an equation to express the total as a sum of equal addends.

data.title: Classify shapes by attributes
data.cluster: Reason with shapes and their attributes.
data.description: Understand that shapes in different categories (e.g., rhombuses, rectangles, and others) may share attributes (e.g., having four sides), and that the shared attributes can define a larger category (e.g., quadrilaterals). Recognize rhombuses, rectangles, and squares as examples of quadrilaterals, and draw examples of quadrilaterals that do not belong to any of these subcategories.

data.title: Partition shapes into parts with equal areas
data.cluster: Reason with shapes and their attributes.
data.description: Partition shapes into parts with equal areas. Express the area of each part as a unit fraction of the whole. For example, partition a shape into 4 parts with equal area, and describe the area of each part as 1/4 of the area of the shape.

data.title: Tell and write time and solve time interval problems
data.cluster: Solve problems involving measurement and estimation of intervals of time, liquid volumes, and masses of objects.
data.description: Tell and write time to the nearest minute and measure time intervals in minutes. Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of time intervals in minutes, e.g., by representing the problem on a number line diagram.

data.title: Measure and estimate liquid volumes and masses
data.cluster: Solve problems involving measurement and estimation of intervals of time, liquid volumes, and masses of objects.
data.description: Measure and estimate liquid volumes and masses of objects using standard units of grams (g), kilograms (kg), and liters (l). Add, subtract, multiply, or divide to solve one-step word problems involving masses or volumes that are given in the same units, e.g., by using drawings (such as a beaker with a measurement scale) to represent the problem.

data.title: Draw scaled picture and bar graphs
data.description: Draw a scaled picture graph and a scaled bar graph to represent a data set with several categories. Solve one- and two-step “how many more” and “how many less” problems using information presented in scaled bar graphs. For example, draw a bar graph in which each square in the bar graph might represent 5 pets.

data.title: Generate measurement data and make line plots
data.description: Generate measurement data by measuring lengths using rulers marked with halves and fourths of an inch. Show the data by making a line plot, where the horizontal scale is marked off in appropriate units—whole numbers, halves, or quarters.

data.title: Understand unit squares and area
data.cluster: Geometric measurement: understand concepts of area and relate area to multiplication and to addition.
data.description: A square with side length 1 unit, called “a unit square,” is said to have “one square unit” of area, and can be used to measure area.

data.title: Understand area of plane figures
data.cluster: Geometric measurement: understand concepts of area and relate area to multiplication and to addition.
data.description: A plane figure which can be covered without gaps or overlaps by n unit squares is said to have an area of n square units.

data.title: Measure areas by counting unit squares
data.cluster: Geometric measurement: understand concepts of area and relate area to multiplication and to addition.
data.description: Measure areas by counting unit squares (square cm, square m, square in, square ft, and improvised units).

data.title: Find area of rectangles by tiling
data.cluster: Geometric measurement: understand concepts of area and relate area to multiplication and to addition.
data.description: Find the area of a rectangle with whole-number side lengths by tiling it, and show that the area is the same as would be found by multiplying the side lengths.

data.title: Multiply side lengths to find area of rectangles
data.cluster: Geometric measurement: understand concepts of area and relate area to multiplication and to addition.
data.description: Multiply side lengths to find areas of rectangles with whole-number side lengths in the context of solving real world and mathematical problems, and represent whole-number products as rectangular areas in mathematical reasoning.

data.title: Use area models for distributive property
data.cluster: Geometric measurement: understand concepts of area and relate area to multiplication and to addition.
data.description: Use tiling to show in a concrete case that the area of a rectangle with whole-number side lengths a and b + c is the sum of a × b and a × c. Use area models to represent the distributive property in mathematical reasoning.

data.title: Recognize area as additive and find area of rectilinear figures
data.cluster: Geometric measurement: understand concepts of area and relate area to multiplication and to addition.
data.description: Recognize area as additive. Find areas of rectilinear figures by decomposing them into non-overlapping rectangles and adding the areas of the non-overlapping parts, applying this technique to solve real world problems.

data.title: Solve problems involving perimeters
data.cluster: Geometric measurement: recognize perimeter as an attribute of plane figures and distinguish between linear and area measures.
data.description: Solve real world and mathematical problems involving perimeters of polygons, including finding the perimeter given the side lengths, finding an unknown side length, and exhibiting rectangles with the same perimeter and different areas or with the same area and different perimeters.

data.title: Round whole numbers to the nearest 10 or 100
data.cluster: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to perform multi-digit arithmetic.
data.description: Use place value understanding to round whole numbers to the nearest 10 or 100.

data.title: Fluently add and subtract within 1000
data.cluster: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to perform multi-digit arithmetic.
data.description: Fluently add and subtract within 1000 using strategies and algorithms based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction.

data.title: Multiply one-digit whole numbers by multiples of 10
data.cluster: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to perform multi-digit arithmetic.
data.description: Multiply one-digit whole numbers by multiples of 10 in the range 10–90 (e.g., 9 × 80, 5 × 60) using strategies based on place value and properties of operations.

data.title: Understand fractions as parts of a whole
data.cluster: Develop understanding of fractions as numbers.
data.description: Understand a fraction 1/b as the quantity formed by 1 part when a whole is partitioned into b equal parts; understand a fraction a/b as the quantity formed by a parts of size 1/b.

data.title: Represent a fraction 1/b on a number line diagram
data.cluster: Develop understanding of fractions as numbers.
data.description: Represent a fraction 1/b on a number line diagram by defining the interval from 0 to 1 as the whole and partitioning it into b equal parts. Recognize that each part has size 1/b and that the endpoint of the part based at 0 locates the number 1/b on the number line.

data.title: Represent a fraction a/b on a number line diagram
data.cluster: Develop understanding of fractions as numbers.
data.description: Represent a fraction a/b on a number line diagram by marking off a lengths 1/b from 0. Recognize that the resulting interval has size a/b and that its endpoint locates the number a/b on the number line.

data.title: Understand equivalent fractions
data.cluster: Develop understanding of fractions as numbers.
data.description: Understand two fractions as equivalent (equal) if they are the same size, or the same point on a number line.

data.title: Recognize and generate simple equivalent fractions
data.cluster: Develop understanding of fractions as numbers.
data.description: Recognize and generate simple equivalent fractions, e.g., 1/2 = 2/4, 4/6 = 2/3. Explain why the fractions are equivalent, e.g., by using a visual fraction model.

data.title: Express whole numbers as fractions
data.cluster: Develop understanding of fractions as numbers.
data.description: Express whole numbers as fractions, and recognize fractions that are equivalent to whole numbers. Examples: Express 3 in the form 3 = 3/1; recognize that 6/1 = 6; locate 4/4 and 1 at the same point of a number line diagram.

data.title: Compare two fractions with the same numerator or denominator
data.cluster: Develop understanding of fractions as numbers.
data.description: Compare two fractions with the same numerator or the same denominator by reasoning about their size. Recognize that comparisons are valid only when the two fractions refer to the same whole. Record the results of comparisons with the symbols >, =, or <, and justify the conclusions, e.g., by using a visual fraction model.

data.title: Interpret products of whole numbers
data.cluster: Represent and solve problems involving multiplication and division.
data.description: Interpret products of whole numbers, e.g., interpret 5 × 7 as the total number of objects in 5 groups of 7 objects each. For example, describe a context in which a total number of objects can be expressed as 5 × 7.

data.title: Interpret whole-number quotients
data.cluster: Represent and solve problems involving multiplication and division.
data.description: Interpret whole-number quotients of whole numbers, e.g., interpret 56 ÷ 8 as the number of objects in each share when 56 objects are partitioned equally into 8 shares, or as a number of shares when 56 objects are partitioned into equal shares of 8 objects each. For example, describe a context in which a number of shares or a number of groups can be expressed as 56 ÷ 8.

data.title: Solve multiplication and division word problems
data.cluster: Represent and solve problems involving multiplication and division.
data.description: Use multiplication and division within 100 to solve word problems in situations involving equal groups, arrays, and measurement quantities, e.g., by using drawings and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.

data.title: Determine unknown whole numbers in multiplication and division equations
data.cluster: Represent and solve problems involving multiplication and division.
data.description: Determine the unknown whole number in a multiplication or division equation relating three whole numbers. For example, determine the unknown number that makes the equation true in each of the equations 8 × ? = 48, 5 = _ ÷ 3, 6 × 6 = ?.

data.title: Apply properties of operations as strategies to multiply and divide
data.cluster: Understand properties of multiplication and the relationship between multiplication and division.
data.description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to multiply and divide. Examples: If 6 × 4 = 24 is known, then 4 × 6 = 24 is also known. (Commutative property of multiplication.) 3 × 5 × 2 can be found by 3 × 5 = 15, then 15 × 2 = 30, or by 5 × 2 = 10, then 3 × 10 = 30. (Associative property of multiplication.) Knowing that 8 × 5 = 40 and 8 × 2 = 16, one can find 8 × 7 as 8 × (5 + 2) = (8 × 5) + (8 × 2) = 40 + 16 = 56. (Distributive property.)

data.title: Understand division as an unknown-factor problem
data.cluster: Understand properties of multiplication and the relationship between multiplication and division.
data.description: Understand division as an unknown-factor problem. For example, find 32 ÷ 8 by finding the number that makes 32 when multiplied by 8.

data.title: Fluently multiply and divide within 100
data.description: Fluently multiply and divide within 100, using strategies such as the relationship between multiplication and division (e.g., knowing that 8 × 5 = 40, one knows 40 ÷ 5 = 8) or properties of operations. By the end of Grade 3, know from memory all products of two one-digit numbers.

data.title: Solve two-step word problems using the four operations
data.cluster: Solve problems involving the four operations, and identify and explain patterns in arithmetic.
data.description: Solve two-step word problems using the four operations. Represent these problems using equations with a letter standing for the unknown quantity. Assess the reasonableness of answers using mental computation and estimation strategies including rounding.

data.title: Identify and explain arithmetic patterns
data.cluster: Solve problems involving the four operations, and identify and explain patterns in arithmetic.
data.description: Identify arithmetic patterns (including patterns in the addition table or multiplication table), and explain them using properties of operations. For example, observe that 4 times a number is always even, and explain why 4 times a number can be decomposed into two equal addends.

data.title: Draw and identify lines and angles
data.cluster: Draw and identify lines and angles, and classify shapes by properties of their lines and angles.
data.description: Draw points, lines, line segments, rays, angles (right, acute, obtuse), and perpendicular and parallel lines. Identify these in two-dimensional figures.

data.title: Classify shapes by lines and angles
data.cluster: Draw and identify lines and angles, and classify shapes by properties of their lines and angles.
data.description: Classify two-dimensional figures based on the presence or absence of parallel or perpendicular lines, or the presence or absence of angles of a specified size. Recognize right triangles as a category, and identify right triangles.

data.title: Lines of symmetry
data.cluster: Draw and identify lines and angles, and classify shapes by properties of their lines and angles.
data.description: Recognize a line of symmetry for a two-dimensional figure as a line across the figure such that the figure can be folded along the line into matching parts. Identify line-symmetric figures and draw lines of symmetry.

data.title: Measurement units and conversions
data.cluster: Solve problems involving measurement and conversion of measurements from a larger unit to a smaller unit.
data.description: Know relative sizes of measurement units within one system of units including km, m, cm; kg, g; lb, oz.; l, ml; hr, min, sec. Within a single system of measurement, express measurements in a larger unit in terms of a smaller unit. Record measurement equivalents in a two-column table. For example, know that 1 ft is 12 times as long as 1 in. Express the length of a 4 ft snake as 48 in. Generate a conversion table for feet and inches listing the number pairs (1, 12), (2, 24), (3, 36), ...

data.title: Word problems with measurements
data.cluster: Solve problems involving measurement and conversion of measurements from a larger unit to a smaller unit.
data.description: Use the four operations to solve word problems involving distances, intervals of time, liquid volumes, masses of objects, and money, including problems involving simple fractions or decimals, and problems that require expressing measurements given in a larger unit in terms of a smaller unit. Represent measurement quantities using diagrams such as number line diagrams that feature a measurement scale.

data.title: Area and perimeter
data.cluster: Solve problems involving measurement and conversion of measurements from a larger unit to a smaller unit.
data.description: Apply the area and perimeter formulas for rectangles in real world and mathematical problems. For example, find the width of a rectangular room given the area of the flooring and the length, by viewing the area formula as a multiplication equation with an unknown factor.

data.title: Line plots with fractions
data.description: Make a line plot to display a data set of measurements in fractions of a unit (1/2, 1/4, 1/8). Solve problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions by using information presented in line plots. For example, from a line plot find and interpret the difference in length between the longest and shortest specimens in an insect collection.

data.title: Concepts of angle measurement
data.cluster: Geometric measurement: understand concepts of angle and measure angles.
data.description: Recognize angles as geometric shapes that are formed wherever two rays share a common endpoint, and understand concepts of angle measurement.

data.title: Degree measure
data.cluster: Geometric measurement: understand concepts of angle and measure angles.
data.description: An angle is measured with reference to a circle with its center at the common endpoint of the rays, by considering the fraction of the circular arc between the points where the two rays intersect the circle. An angle that turns through 1/360 of a circle is called a “one-degree angle,” and can be used to measure angles.

data.title: Angle measure in degrees
data.cluster: Geometric measurement: understand concepts of angle and measure angles.
data.description: An angle that turns through n one-degree angles is said to have an angle measure of n degrees.

data.title: Measure angles with protractor
data.cluster: Geometric measurement: understand concepts of angle and measure angles.
data.description: Measure angles in whole-number degrees using a protractor. Sketch angles of specified measure.

data.title: Additive nature of angle measure
data.cluster: Geometric measurement: understand concepts of angle and measure angles.
data.description: Recognize angle measure as additive. When an angle is decomposed into non-overlapping parts, the angle measure of the whole is the sum of the angle measures of the parts. Solve addition and subtraction problems to find unknown angles on a diagram in real world and mathematical problems, e.g., by using an equation with a symbol for the unknown angle measure.

data.title: Place value relationships
data.cluster: Generalize place value understanding for multi-digit whole numbers.
data.description: Recognize that in a multi-digit whole number, a digit in one place represents ten times what it represents in the place to its right. For example, recognize that 700 ÷ 70 = 10 by applying concepts of place value and division.

data.title: Read, write, and compare multi-digit numbers
data.cluster: Generalize place value understanding for multi-digit whole numbers.
data.description: Read and write multi-digit whole numbers using base-ten numerals, number names, and expanded form. Compare two multi-digit numbers based on meanings of the digits in each place, using >, =, and < symbols to record the results of comparisons.

data.title: Rounding multi-digit numbers
data.cluster: Generalize place value understanding for multi-digit whole numbers.
data.description: Use place value understanding to round multi-digit whole numbers to any place.

data.title: Add and subtract multi-digit numbers
data.cluster: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to perform multi-digit arithmetic.
data.description: Fluently add and subtract multi-digit whole numbers using the standard algorithm.

data.title: Multiply multi-digit numbers
data.cluster: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to perform multi-digit arithmetic.
data.description: Multiply a whole number of up to four digits by a one-digit whole number, and multiply two two-digit numbers, using strategies based on place value and the properties of operations. Illustrate and explain the calculation by using equations, rectangular arrays, and/or area models.

data.title: Divide multi-digit numbers
data.cluster: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to perform multi-digit arithmetic.
data.description: Find whole-number quotients and remainders with up to four-digit dividends and one-digit divisors, using strategies based on place value, the properties of operations, and/or the relationship between multiplication and division. Illustrate and explain the calculation by using equations, rectangular arrays, and/or area models.

data.title: Equivalent fractions
data.cluster: Extend understanding of fraction equivalence and ordering.
data.description: Explain why a fraction a/b is equivalent to a fraction (n × a)/(n × b) by using visual fraction models, with attention to how the number and size of the parts differ even though the two fractions themselves are the same size. Use this principle to recognize and generate equivalent fractions.

data.title: Compare fractions
data.cluster: Extend understanding of fraction equivalence and ordering.
data.description: Compare two fractions with different numerators and different denominators, e.g., by creating common denominators or numerators, or by comparing to a benchmark fraction such as 1/2. Recognize that comparisons are valid only when the two fractions refer to the same whole. Record the results of comparisons with symbols >, =, or <, and justify the conclusions, e.g., by using a visual fraction model.

data.title: Fractions as sums of unit fractions
data.cluster: Build fractions from unit fractions by applying and extending previous understandings of operations on whole numbers.
data.description: Understand a fraction a/b with a > 1 as a sum of fractions 1/b.

data.title: Add/subtract fractions as joining/separating
data.cluster: Build fractions from unit fractions by applying and extending previous understandings of operations on whole numbers.
data.description: Understand addition and subtraction of fractions as joining and separating parts referring to the same whole.

data.title: Decompose fractions
data.cluster: Build fractions from unit fractions by applying and extending previous understandings of operations on whole numbers.
data.description: Decompose a fraction into a sum of fractions with the same denominator in more than one way, recording each decomposition by an equation. Justify decompositions, e.g., by using a visual fraction model. Examples: 3/8 = 1/8 + 1/8 + 1/8 ; 3/8 = 1/8 + 2/8 ; 2 1/8 = 1 + 1 + 1/8 = 8/8 + 8/8 + 1/8.

data.title: Add/subtract mixed numbers
data.cluster: Build fractions from unit fractions by applying and extending previous understandings of operations on whole numbers.
data.description: Add and subtract mixed numbers with like denominators, e.g., by replacing each mixed number with an equivalent fraction, and/or by using properties of operations and the relationship between addition and subtraction.

data.title: Word problems with fractions
data.cluster: Build fractions from unit fractions by applying and extending previous understandings of operations on whole numbers.
data.description: Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions referring to the same whole and having like denominators, e.g., by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem.

data.title: Multiply fraction by whole number
data.cluster: Build fractions from unit fractions by applying and extending previous understandings of operations on whole numbers.
data.description: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication to multiply a fraction by a whole number.

data.title: Fraction as multiple of unit fraction
data.cluster: Build fractions from unit fractions by applying and extending previous understandings of operations on whole numbers.
data.description: Understand a fraction a/b as a multiple of 1/b. For example, use a visual fraction model to represent 5/4 as the product 5 × (1/4), recording the conclusion by the equation 5/4 = 5 × (1/4).

data.title: Multiple of fraction as multiple of unit fraction
data.cluster: Build fractions from unit fractions by applying and extending previous understandings of operations on whole numbers.
data.description: Understand a multiple of a/b as a multiple of 1/b, and use this understanding to multiply a fraction by a whole number. For example, use a visual fraction model to express 3 × (2/5) as 6 × (1/5), recognizing this product as 6/5. (In general, n × (a/b) = (n × a)/b.)

data.title: Word problems: multiply fraction by whole number
data.cluster: Build fractions from unit fractions by applying and extending previous understandings of operations on whole numbers.
data.description: Solve word problems involving multiplication of a fraction by a whole number, e.g., by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem. For example, if each person at a party will eat 3/8 of a pound of roast beef, and there will be 5 people at the party, how many pounds of roast beef will be needed? Between what two whole numbers does your answer lie?

data.title: Fractions with denominators 10 and 100
data.cluster: Understand decimal notation for fractions, and compare decimal fractions.
data.description: Express a fraction with denominator 10 as an equivalent fraction with denominator 100, and use this technique to add two fractions with respective denominators 10 and 100. For example, express 3/10 as 30/100, and add 3/10 + 4/100 = 34/100.

data.title: Decimal notation for fractions
data.cluster: Understand decimal notation for fractions, and compare decimal fractions.
data.description: Use decimal notation for fractions with denominators 10 or 100. For example, rewrite 0.62 as 62/100; describe a length as 0.62 meters; locate 0.62 on a number line diagram.

data.title: Compare decimals
data.cluster: Understand decimal notation for fractions, and compare decimal fractions.
data.description: Compare two decimals to hundredths by reasoning about their size. Recognize that comparisons are valid only when the two decimals refer to the same whole. Record the results of comparisons with the symbols >, =, or <, and justify the conclusions, e.g., by using a visual model.

data.title: Interpret multiplication as comparison
data.cluster: Use the four operations with whole numbers to solve problems.
data.description: Interpret a multiplication equation as a comparison, e.g., interpret 35 = 5 × 7 as a statement that 35 is 5 times as many as 7 and 7 times as many as 5. Represent verbal statements of multiplicative comparisons as multiplication equations.

data.title: Word problems with multiplicative comparison
data.cluster: Use the four operations with whole numbers to solve problems.
data.description: Multiply or divide to solve word problems involving multiplicative comparison, e.g., by using drawings and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem, distinguishing multiplicative comparison from additive comparison.

data.title: Multi-step word problems
data.cluster: Use the four operations with whole numbers to solve problems.
data.description: Solve multistep word problems posed with whole numbers and having whole-number answers using the four operations, including problems in which remainders must be interpreted. Represent these problems using equations with a letter standing for the unknown quantity. Assess the reasonableness of answers using mental computation and estimation strategies including rounding.

data.title: Factors and multiples
data.cluster: Gain familiarity with factors and multiples.
data.description: Find all factor pairs for a whole number in the range 1–100. Recognize that a whole number is a multiple of each of its factors. Determine whether a given whole number in the range 1–100 is a multiple of a given one-digit number. Determine whether a given whole number in the range 1–100 is prime or composite.

data.title: Generate and analyze patterns
data.description: Generate a number or shape pattern that follows a given rule. Identify apparent features of the pattern that were not explicit in the rule itself. For example, given the rule “Add 3” and the starting number 1, generate terms in the resulting sequence and observe that the terms appear to alternate between odd and even numbers. Explain informally why the numbers will continue to alternate in this way.

data.title: Coordinate System
data.cluster: Graph points on the coordinate plane to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
data.description: Use a pair of perpendicular number lines, called axes, to define a coordinate system, with the intersection of the lines (the origin) arranged to coincide with the 0 on each line and a given point in the plane located by using an ordered pair of numbers, called its coordinates. Understand that the first number indicates how far to travel from the origin in the direction of one axis, and the second number indicates how far to travel in the direction of the second axis, with the convention that the names of the two axes and the coordinates correspond (e.g., x-axis and x-coordinate, y-axis and y-coordinate).

data.title: Graph Points in First Quadrant
data.cluster: Graph points on the coordinate plane to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
data.description: Represent real world and mathematical problems by graphing points in the first quadrant of the coordinate plane, and interpret coordinate values of points in the context of the situation.

data.title: Classify Two-Dimensional Figures
data.cluster: Classify two-dimensional figures into categories based on their properties.
data.description: Understand that attributes belonging to a category of two-dimensional figures also belong to all subcategories of that category. For example, all rectangles have four right angles and squares are rectangles, so all squares have four right angles.

data.title: Hierarchy of Two-Dimensional Figures
data.cluster: Classify two-dimensional figures into categories based on their properties.
data.description: Classify two-dimensional figures in a hierarchy based on properties.

data.title: Convert Measurement Units
data.cluster: Convert like measurement units within a given measurement system.
data.description: Convert among different-sized standard measurement units within a given measurement system (e.g., convert 5 cm to 0.05 m), and use these conversions in solving multi-step, real world problems.

data.title: Represent and Interpret Data
data.description: Make a line plot to display a data set of measurements in fractions of a unit (1/2, 1/4, 1/8). Use operations on fractions for this grade to solve problems involving information presented in line plots. For example, given different measurements of liquid in identical beakers, find the amount of liquid each beaker would contain if the total amount in all the beakers were redistributed equally.

data.title: Concepts of Volume Measurement
data.cluster: Geometric measurement: understand concepts of volume and relate volume to multiplication and to addition.
data.description: Recognize volume as an attribute of solid figures and understand concepts of volume measurement.

data.title: Unit Cube and Volume
data.cluster: Geometric measurement: understand concepts of volume and relate volume to multiplication and to addition.
data.description: A cube with side length 1 unit, called a “unit cube,” is said to have “one cubic unit” of volume, and can be used to measure volume.

data.title: Volume of Solid Figures
data.cluster: Geometric measurement: understand concepts of volume and relate volume to multiplication and to addition.
data.description: A solid figure which can be packed without gaps or overlaps using n unit cubes is said to have a volume of n cubic units.

data.title: Measure Volume by Counting
data.cluster: Geometric measurement: understand concepts of volume and relate volume to multiplication and to addition.
data.description: Measure volumes by counting unit cubes, using cubic cm, cubic in, cubic ft, and improvised units.

data.title: Relate Volume to Multiplication and Addition
data.cluster: Geometric measurement: understand concepts of volume and relate volume to multiplication and to addition.
data.description: Relate volume to the operations of multiplication and addition and solve real world and mathematical problems involving volume.

data.title: Volume of Right Rectangular Prisms
data.cluster: Geometric measurement: understand concepts of volume and relate volume to multiplication and to addition.
data.description: Find the volume of a right rectangular prism with whole-number side lengths by packing it with unit cubes, and show that the volume is the same as would be found by multiplying the edge lengths, equivalently by multiplying the height by the area of the base. Represent threefold whole-number products as volumes, e.g., to represent the associative property of multiplication.

data.title: Apply Volume Formulas
data.cluster: Geometric measurement: understand concepts of volume and relate volume to multiplication and to addition.
data.description: Apply the formulas V = l × w × h and V = b × h for rectangular prisms to find volumes of right rectangular prisms with whole-number edge lengths in the context of solving real world and mathematical problems.

data.title: Volume of Composite Figures
data.cluster: Geometric measurement: understand concepts of volume and relate volume to multiplication and to addition.
data.description: Recognize volume as additive. Find volumes of solid figures composed of two non-overlapping right rectangular prisms by adding the volumes of the non-overlapping parts, applying this technique to solve real world problems.

data.title: Place Value in Multi-Digit Numbers
data.description: Recognize that in a multi-digit number, a digit in one place represents 10 times as much as it represents in the place to its right and 1/10 of what it represents in the place to its left.

data.title: Powers of 10 and Decimal Patterns
data.description: Explain patterns in the number of zeros of the product when multiplying a number by powers of 10, and explain patterns in the placement of the decimal point when a decimal is multiplied or divided by a power of 10. Use whole-number exponents to denote powers of 10.

data.title: Read, Write, and Compare Decimals
data.description: Read, write, and compare decimals to thousandths.

data.title: Read and Write Decimals
data.description: Read and write decimals to thousandths using base-ten numerals, number names, and expanded form, e.g., 347.392 = 3 × 100 + 4 × 10 + 7 × 1 + 3 × (1/10) + 9 × (1/100) + 2 × (1/1000).

data.title: Compare Decimals
data.description: Compare two decimals to thousandths based on meanings of the digits in each place, using >, =, and < symbols to record the results of comparisons.

data.title: Round Decimals
data.description: Use place value understanding to round decimals to any place.

data.title: Multiply Multi-Digit Whole Numbers
data.cluster: Perform operations with multi-digit whole numbers and with decimals to hundredths.
data.description: Fluently multiply multi-digit whole numbers using the standard algorithm.

data.title: Divide Whole Numbers
data.cluster: Perform operations with multi-digit whole numbers and with decimals to hundredths.
data.description: Find whole-number quotients of whole numbers with up to four-digit dividends and two-digit divisors, using strategies based on place value, the properties of operations, and/or the relationship between multiplication and division. Illustrate and explain the calculation by using equations, rectangular arrays, and/or area models.

data.title: Operations with Decimals
data.cluster: Perform operations with multi-digit whole numbers and with decimals to hundredths.
data.description: Add, subtract, multiply, and divide decimals to hundredths, using concrete models or drawings and strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction; relate the strategy to a written method and explain the reasoning used.

data.title: Add and Subtract Fractions with Unlike Denominators
data.cluster: Use equivalent fractions as a strategy to add and subtract fractions.
data.description: Add and subtract fractions with unlike denominators (including mixed numbers) by replacing given fractions with equivalent fractions in such a way as to produce an equivalent sum or difference of fractions with like denominators. For example, 2/3 + 5/4 = 8/12 + 15/12 = 23/12. (In general, a/b + c/d = (ad + bc)/bd.)

data.title: Word Problems: Add and Subtract Fractions
data.cluster: Use equivalent fractions as a strategy to add and subtract fractions.
data.description: Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions referring to the same whole, including cases of unlike denominators, e.g., by using visual fraction models or equations to represent the problem. Use benchmark fractions and number sense of fractions to estimate mentally and assess the reasonableness of answers. For example, recognize an incorrect result 2/5 + 1/2 = 3/7, by observing that 3/7 < 1/2.

data.title: Fractions as Division
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication and division to multiply and divide fractions.
data.description: Interpret a fraction as division of the numerator by the denominator (a/b = a ÷ b). Solve word problems involving division of whole numbers leading to answers in the form of fractions or mixed numbers, e.g., by using visual fraction models or equations to represent the problem. For example, interpret 3/4 as the result of dividing 3 by 4, noting that 3/4 multiplied by 4 equals 3, and that when 3 wholes are shared equally among 4 people each person has a share of size 3/4. If 9 people want to share a 50-pound sack of rice equally by weight, how many pounds of rice should each person get? Between what two whole numbers does your answer lie?

data.title: Multiply Fractions
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication and division to multiply and divide fractions.
data.description: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication to multiply a fraction or whole number by a fraction.

data.title: Interpret Fraction Multiplication
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication and division to multiply and divide fractions.
data.description: Interpret the product (a/b) × q as a parts of a partition of q into b equal parts; equivalently, as the result of a sequence of operations a × q ÷ b. For example, use a visual fraction model to show (2/3) × 4 = 8/3, and create a story context for this equation. Do the same with (2/3) × (4/5) = 8/15. (In general, (a/b) × (c/d) = ac/bd.)

data.title: Area of Rectangles with Fractional Sides
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication and division to multiply and divide fractions.
data.description: Find the area of a rectangle with fractional side lengths by tiling it with unit squares of the appropriate unit fraction side lengths, and show that the area is the same as would be found by multiplying the side lengths. Multiply fractional side lengths to find areas of rectangles, and represent fraction products as rectangular areas.

data.title: Multiplication as Scaling
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication and division to multiply and divide fractions.
data.description: Interpret multiplication as scaling (resizing).

data.title: Compare Product Size to Factors
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication and division to multiply and divide fractions.
data.description: Comparing the size of a product to the size of one factor on the basis of the size of the other factor, without performing the indicated multiplication.

data.title: Explain Multiplication as Scaling
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication and division to multiply and divide fractions.
data.description: Explaining why multiplying a given number by a fraction greater than 1 results in a product greater than the given number (recognizing multiplication by whole numbers greater than 1 as a familiar case); explaining why multiplying a given number by a fraction less than 1 results in a product smaller than the given number; and relating the principle of fraction equivalence a/b = (n×a)/(n×b) to the effect of multiplying a/b by 1.

data.title: Word Problems: Multiply Fractions
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication and division to multiply and divide fractions.
data.description: Solve real world problems involving multiplication of fractions and mixed numbers, e.g., by using visual fraction models or equations to represent the problem.

data.title: Divide Unit Fractions and Whole Numbers
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication and division to multiply and divide fractions.
data.description: Apply and extend previous understandings of division to divide unit fractions by whole numbers and whole numbers by unit fractions.

data.title: Divide Unit Fraction by Whole Number
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication and division to multiply and divide fractions.
data.description: Interpret division of a unit fraction by a non-zero whole number, and compute such quotients. For example, create a story context for (1/3) ÷ 4, and use a visual fraction model to show the quotient. Use the relationship between multiplication and division to explain that (1/3) ÷ 4 = 1/12 because (1/12) × 4 = 1/3.

data.title: Divide Whole Number by Unit Fraction
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication and division to multiply and divide fractions.
data.description: Interpret division of a whole number by a unit fraction, and compute such quotients. For example, create a story context for 4 ÷ (1/5), and use a visual fraction model to show the quotient. Use the relationship between multiplication and division to explain that 4 ÷ (1/5) = 20 because 20 × (1/5) = 4.

data.title: Word Problems: Division of Fractions
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication and division to multiply and divide fractions.
data.description: Solve real world problems involving division of unit fractions by non-zero whole numbers and division of whole numbers by unit fractions, e.g., by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem. For example, how much chocolate will each person get if 3 people share 1/2 lb of chocolate equally? How many 1/3-cup servings are in 2 cups of raisins?

data.title: Evaluate Numerical Expressions
data.description: Use parentheses, brackets, or braces in numerical expressions, and evaluate expressions with these symbols.

data.title: Write and Interpret Numerical Expressions
data.description: Write simple expressions that record calculations with numbers, and interpret numerical expressions without evaluating them. For example, express the calculation “add 8 and 7, then multiply by 2” as 2 × (8 + 7). Recognize that 3 × (18932 + 921) is three times as large as 18932 + 921, without having to calculate the indicated sum or product.

data.title: Analyze Patterns and Relationships
data.description: Generate two numerical patterns using two given rules. Identify apparent relationships between corresponding terms. Form ordered pairs consisting of corresponding terms from the two patterns, and graph the ordered pairs on a coordinate plane. For example, given the rule “Add 3” and the starting number 0, and given the rule “Add 6” and the starting number 0, generate terms in the resulting sequences, and observe that the terms in one sequence are twice the corresponding terms in the other sequence. Explain informally why this is so.

data.title: Write and Evaluate Numerical Expressions
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of arithmetic to algebraic expressions.
data.description: Write and evaluate numerical expressions involving whole-number exponents.

data.title: Write, Read, and Evaluate Expressions
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of arithmetic to algebraic expressions.
data.description: Write, read, and evaluate expressions in which letters stand for numbers.

data.title: Write Expressions Recording Operations
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of arithmetic to algebraic expressions.
data.description: Write expressions that record operations with numbers and with letters standing for numbers. For example, express the calculation "Subtract y from 5" as 5 – y.

data.title: Identify Parts of an Expression
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of arithmetic to algebraic expressions.
data.description: Identify parts of an expression using mathematical terms (sum, term, product, factor, quotient, coefficient); view one or more parts of an expression as a single entity. For example, describe the expression 2 (8 + 7) as a product of two factors; view (8 + 7) as both a single entity and a sum of two terms.

data.title: Evaluate Expressions at Specific Values
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of arithmetic to algebraic expressions.
data.description: Evaluate expressions at specific values of their variables. Include expressions that arise from formulas used in real-world problems. Perform arithmetic operations, including those involving whole-number exponents, in the conventional order when there are no parentheses to specify a particular order (Order of Operations). For example, use the formulas V = s³ and A = 6s² to find the volume and surface area of a cube with sides of length s = 1/2.

data.title: Apply Properties of Operations
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of arithmetic to algebraic expressions.
data.description: Apply the properties of operations to generate equivalent expressions. For example, apply the distributive property to the expression 3 (2 + x) to produce the equivalent expression 6 + 3x; apply the distributive property to the expression 24x + 18y to produce the equivalent expression 6 (4x + 3y); apply properties of operations to y + y + y to produce the equivalent expression 3y.

data.title: Identify Equivalent Expressions
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of arithmetic to algebraic expressions.
data.description: Identify when two expressions are equivalent (i.e., when the two expressions name the same number regardless of which value is substituted into them). For example, the expressions y + y + y and 3y are equivalent because they name the same number regardless of which number y stands for.

data.title: Understand Solving Equations and Inequalities
data.cluster: Reason about and solve one-variable equations and inequalities.
data.description: Understand solving an equation or inequality as a process of answering a question: which values from a specified set, if any, make the equation or inequality true? Use substitution to determine whether a given number in a specified set makes an equation or inequality true.

data.title: Use Variables to Represent Numbers
data.cluster: Reason about and solve one-variable equations and inequalities.
data.description: Use variables to represent numbers and write expressions when solving a real-world or mathematical problem; understand that a variable can represent an unknown number, or, depending on the purpose at hand, any number in a specified set.

data.title: Solve Real-World and Mathematical Problems
data.cluster: Reason about and solve one-variable equations and inequalities.
data.description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems by writing and solving equations of the form x + p = q and px = q for cases in which p, q and x are all nonnegative rational numbers.

data.title: Write Inequalities
data.cluster: Reason about and solve one-variable equations and inequalities.
data.description: Write an inequality of the form x > c or x < c to represent a constraint or condition in a real-world or mathematical problem. Recognize that inequalities of the form x > c or x < c have infinitely many solutions; represent solutions of such inequalities on number line diagrams.

data.title: Use Variables to Represent Two Quantities
data.cluster: Represent and analyze quantitative relationships between dependent and independent variables.
data.description: Use variables to represent two quantities in a real-world problem that change in relationship to one another; write an equation to express one quantity, thought of as the dependent variable, in terms of the other quantity, thought of as the independent variable. Analyze the relationship between the dependent and independent variables using graphs and tables, and relate these to the equation. For example, in a problem involving motion at constant speed, list and graph ordered pairs of distances and times, and write the equation d = 65t to represent the relationship between distance and time.
