data.title: Find Area of Polygons
data.cluster: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving area, surface area, and volume.
data.description: Find the area of right triangles, other triangles, special quadrilaterals, and polygons by composing into rectangles or decomposing into triangles and other shapes; apply these techniques in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems.

data.title: Find Volume of Right Rectangular Prisms
data.cluster: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving area, surface area, and volume.
data.description: Find the volume of a right rectangular prism with fractional edge lengths by packing it with unit cubes of the appropriate unit fraction edge lengths, and show that the volume is the same as would be found by multiplying the edge lengths of the prism. Apply the formulas V = l w h and V = b h to find volumes of right rectangular prisms with fractional edge lengths in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems.

data.title: Draw Polygons in Coordinate Plane
data.cluster: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving area, surface area, and volume.
data.description: Draw polygons in the coordinate plane given coordinates for the vertices; use coordinates to find the length of a side joining points with the same first coordinate or the same second coordinate. Apply these techniques in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems.

data.title: Represent 3D Figures Using Nets
data.cluster: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving area, surface area, and volume.
data.description: Represent three-dimensional figures using nets made up of rectangles and triangles, and use the nets to find the surface area of these figures. Apply these techniques in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems.

data.title: Interpret and Compute Quotients of Fractions
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication and division to divide fractions by fractions.
data.description: Interpret and compute quotients of fractions, and solve word problems involving division of fractions by fractions, e.g., by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem. For example, create a story context for (2/3) ÷ (3/4) and use a visual fraction model to show the quotient; use the relationship between multiplication and division to explain that (2/3) ÷ (3/4) = 8/9 because 3/4 of 8/9 is 2/3. (In general, (a/b) ÷ (c/d) = ad/bc.) How much chocolate will each person get if 3 people share 1/2 lb of chocolate equally? How many 3/4-cup servings are in 2/3 of a cup of yogurt? How wide is a rectangular strip of land with length 3/4 mi and area 1/2 square mi?

data.title: Fluently Divide Multi-Digit Numbers
data.cluster: Compute fluently with multi-digit numbers and find common factors and multiples.
data.description: Fluently divide multi-digit numbers using the standard algorithm.

data.title: Fluently Compute with Multi-Digit Decimals
data.cluster: Compute fluently with multi-digit numbers and find common factors and multiples.
data.description: Fluently add, subtract, multiply, and divide multi-digit decimals using the standard algorithm for each operation.

data.title: Find Greatest Common Factor and Least Common Multiple
data.cluster: Compute fluently with multi-digit numbers and find common factors and multiples.
data.description: Find the greatest common factor of two whole numbers less than or equal to 100 and the least common multiple of two whole numbers less than or equal to 12. Use the distributive property to express a sum of two whole numbers 1–100 with a common factor as a multiple of a sum of two whole numbers with no common factor. For example, express 36 + 8 as 4 (9 + 2).

data.title: Understand Positive and Negative Numbers
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of numbers to the system of rational numbers.
data.description: Understand that positive and negative numbers are used together to describe quantities having opposite directions or values (e.g., temperature above/below zero, elevation above/below sea level, credits/debits, positive/negative electric charge); use positive and negative numbers to represent quantities in real-world contexts, explaining the meaning of 0 in each situation.

data.title: Understand Rational Numbers on Number Line
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of numbers to the system of rational numbers.
data.description: Understand a rational number as a point on the number line. Extend number line diagrams and coordinate axes familiar from previous grades to represent points on the line and in the plane with negative number coordinates.

data.title: Recognize Opposite Signs
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of numbers to the system of rational numbers.
data.description: Recognize opposite signs of numbers as indicating locations on opposite sides of 0 on the number line; recognize that the opposite of the opposite of a number is the number itself, e.g., –(–3) = 3, and that 0 is its own opposite.

data.title: Understand Signs in Ordered Pairs
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of numbers to the system of rational numbers.
data.description: Understand signs of numbers in ordered pairs as indicating locations in quadrants of the coordinate plane; recognize that when two ordered pairs differ only by signs, the locations of the points are related by reflections across one or both axes.

data.title: Find and Position Integers and Rational Numbers
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of numbers to the system of rational numbers.
data.description: Find and position integers and other rational numbers on a horizontal or vertical number line diagram; find and position pairs of integers and other rational numbers on a coordinate plane.

data.title: Understand Ordering and Absolute Value
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of numbers to the system of rational numbers.
data.description: Understand ordering and absolute value of rational numbers.

data.title: Interpret Statements of Inequality
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of numbers to the system of rational numbers.
data.description: Interpret statements of inequality as statements about the relative position of two numbers on a number line diagram. For example, interpret –3 > –7 as a statement that –3 is located to the right of –7 on a number line oriented from left to right.

data.title: Write and Interpret Statements of Order
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of numbers to the system of rational numbers.
data.description: Write, interpret, and explain statements of order for rational numbers in real-world contexts. For example, write –3 °C > –7 °C to express the fact that –3 °C is warmer than –7 °C.

data.title: Understand Absolute Value
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of numbers to the system of rational numbers.
data.description: Understand the absolute value of a rational number as its distance from 0 on the number line; interpret absolute value as magnitude for a positive or negative quantity in a real-world situation. For example, for an account balance of –30 dollars, write |–30| = 30 to describe the size of the debt in dollars.

data.title: Distinguish Comparisons of Absolute Value
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of numbers to the system of rational numbers.
data.description: Distinguish comparisons of absolute value from statements about order. For example, recognize that an account balance less than –30 dollars represents a debt greater than 30 dollars.

data.title: Solve Problems by Graphing Points
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of numbers to the system of rational numbers.
data.description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems by graphing points in all four quadrants of the coordinate plane. Include use of coordinates and absolute value to find distances between points with the same first coordinate or the same second coordinate.

data.title: Understand Ratio Concepts
data.cluster: Understand ratio concepts and use ratio reasoning to solve problems.
data.description: Understand the concept of a ratio and use ratio language to describe a ratio relationship between two quantities. For example, "The ratio of wings to beaks in the bird house at the zoo was 2:1, because for every 2 wings there was 1 beak." "For every vote candidate A received, candidate C received nearly three votes."

data.title: Understand Unit Rate
data.cluster: Understand ratio concepts and use ratio reasoning to solve problems.
data.description: Understand the concept of a unit rate a/b associated with a ratio a:b with b ≠ 0, and use rate language in the context of a ratio relationship. For example, "This recipe has a ratio of 3 cups of flour to 4 cups of sugar, so there is 3/4 cup of flour for each cup of sugar." "We paid $75 for 15 hamburgers, which is a rate of $5 per hamburger."

data.title: Use Ratio and Rate Reasoning
data.cluster: Understand ratio concepts and use ratio reasoning to solve problems.
data.description: Use ratio and rate reasoning to solve real-world and mathematical problems, e.g., by reasoning about tables of equivalent ratios, tape diagrams, double number line diagrams, or equations.

data.title: Make Tables of Equivalent Ratios
data.cluster: Understand ratio concepts and use ratio reasoning to solve problems.
data.description: Make tables of equivalent ratios relating quantities with whole-number measurements, find missing values in the tables, and plot the pairs of values on the coordinate plane. Use tables to compare ratios.

data.title: Solve Unit Rate Problems
data.cluster: Understand ratio concepts and use ratio reasoning to solve problems.
data.description: Solve unit rate problems including those involving unit pricing and constant speed. For example, if it took 7 hours to mow 4 lawns, then at that rate, how many lawns could be mowed in 35 hours? At what rate were lawns being mowed?

data.title: Find a Percent of a Quantity
data.cluster: Understand ratio concepts and use ratio reasoning to solve problems.
data.description: Find a percent of a quantity as a rate per 100 (e.g., 30% of a quantity means 30/100 times the quantity); solve problems involving finding the whole, given a part and the percent.

data.title: Convert Measurement Units
data.cluster: Understand ratio concepts and use ratio reasoning to solve problems.
data.description: Use ratio reasoning to convert measurement units; manipulate and transform units appropriately when multiplying or dividing quantities.

data.title: Recognize Statistical Questions
data.description: Recognize a statistical question as one that anticipates variability in the data related to the question and accounts for it in the answers. For example, "How old am I?" is not a statistical question, but "How old are the students in my school?" is a statistical question because one anticipates variability in students' ages.

data.title: Understand Data Distributions
data.description: Understand that a set of data collected to answer a statistical question has a distribution which can be described by its center, spread, and overall shape.

data.title: Recognize Measures of Center and Variation
data.description: Recognize that a measure of center for a numerical data set summarizes all of its values with a single number, while a measure of variation describes how its values vary with a single number.

data.title: Display Numerical Data
data.description: Display numerical data in plots on a number line, including dot plots, histograms, and box plots.

data.title: Summarize Numerical Data Sets
data.description: Summarize numerical data sets in relation to their context.

data.title: Report Number of Observations
data.description: Reporting the number of observations.

data.title: Describe Nature of Attribute
data.description: Describing the nature of the attribute under investigation, including how it was measured and its units of measurement.

data.title: Give Quantitative Measures
data.description: Giving quantitative measures of center (median and/or mean) and variability (interquartile range and/or mean absolute deviation), as well as describing any overall pattern and any striking deviations from the overall pattern with reference to the context in which the data were gathered.

data.title: Relate Measures to Shape and Context
data.description: Relating the choice of measures of center and variability to the shape of the data distribution and the context in which the data were gathered.

data.title: Apply properties of operations to linear expressions
data.cluster: Use properties of operations to generate equivalent expressions.
data.description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to add, subtract, factor, and expand linear expressions with rational coefficients.

data.title: Understand rewriting expressions in different forms
data.cluster: Use properties of operations to generate equivalent expressions.
data.description: Understand that rewriting an expression in different forms in a problem context can shed light on the problem and how the quantities in it are related. For example, a + 0.05a = 1.05a means that “increase by 5%” is the same as “multiply by 1.05.”

data.title: Solve multi-step real-life and mathematical problems
data.cluster: Solve real-life and mathematical problems using numerical and algebraic expressions and equations.
data.description: Solve multi-step real-life and mathematical problems posed with positive and negative rational numbers in any form (whole numbers, fractions, and decimals), using tools strategically. Apply properties of operations to calculate with numbers in any form; convert between forms as appropriate; and assess the reasonableness of answers using mental computation and estimation strategies. For example: If a woman making $25 an hour gets a 10% raise, she will make an additional 1/10 of her salary an hour, or $2.50, for a new salary of $27.50. If you want to place a towel bar 9 3/4 inches long in the center of a door that is 27 1/2 inches wide, you will need to place the bar about 9 inches from each edge; this estimate can be used as a check on the exact computation.

data.title: Use variables to represent quantities and construct simple equations and inequalities
data.cluster: Solve real-life and mathematical problems using numerical and algebraic expressions and equations.
data.description: Use variables to represent quantities in a real-world or mathematical problem, and construct simple equations and inequalities to solve problems by reasoning about the quantities.

data.title: Solve word problems leading to equations of the form px + q = r and p(x + q) = r
data.cluster: Solve real-life and mathematical problems using numerical and algebraic expressions and equations.
data.description: Solve word problems leading to equations of the form px + q = r and p(x + q) = r, where p, q, and r are specific rational numbers. Solve equations of these forms fluently. Compare an algebraic solution to an arithmetic solution, identifying the sequence of the operations used in each approach. For example, the perimeter of a rectangle is 54 cm. Its length is 6 cm. What is its width?

data.title: Solve word problems leading to inequalities of the form px + q > r or px + q < r
data.cluster: Solve real-life and mathematical problems using numerical and algebraic expressions and equations.
data.description: Solve word problems leading to inequalities of the form px + q > r or px + q < r, where p, q, and r are specific rational numbers. Graph the solution set of the inequality and interpret it in the context of the problem. For example: As a salesperson, you are paid $50 per week plus $3 per sale. This week you want your pay to be at least $100. Write an inequality for the number of sales you need to make, and describe the solutions.

data.title: Solve problems involving scale drawings of geometric figures
data.cluster: Draw, construct, and describe geometrical figures and describe the relationships between them.
data.description: Solve problems involving scale drawings of geometric figures, including computing actual lengths and areas from a scale drawing and reproducing a scale drawing at a different scale.

data.title: Draw geometric shapes with given conditions
data.cluster: Draw, construct, and describe geometrical figures and describe the relationships between them.
data.description: Draw (freehand, with ruler and protractor, and with technology) geometric shapes with given conditions. Focus on constructing triangles from three measures of angles or sides, noticing when the conditions determine a unique triangle, more than one triangle, or no triangle.

data.title: Describe the two-dimensional figures that result from slicing three-dimensional figures
data.cluster: Draw, construct, and describe geometrical figures and describe the relationships between them.
data.description: Describe the two-dimensional figures that result from slicing three-dimensional figures, as in plane sections of right rectangular prisms and right rectangular pyramids.

data.title: Know the formulas for the area and circumference of a circle
data.cluster: Solve real-life and mathematical problems involving angle measure, area, surface area, and volume.
data.description: Know the formulas for the area and circumference of a circle and use them to solve problems; give an informal derivation of the relationship between the circumference and area of a circle.

data.title: Use facts about supplementary, complementary, vertical, and adjacent angles
data.cluster: Solve real-life and mathematical problems involving angle measure, area, surface area, and volume.
data.description: Use facts about supplementary, complementary, vertical, and adjacent angles in a multi-step problem to write and solve simple equations for an unknown angle in a figure.

data.title: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving area, volume and surface area
data.cluster: Solve real-life and mathematical problems involving angle measure, area, surface area, and volume.
data.description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving area, volume and surface area of two- and three-dimensional objects composed of triangles, quadrilaterals, polygons, cubes, and right prisms.

data.title: Apply and extend previous understandings of addition and subtraction to rational numbers
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of operations with fractions to add, subtract, multiply, and divide rational numbers.
data.description: Apply and extend previous understandings of addition and subtraction to add and subtract rational numbers; represent addition and subtraction on a horizontal or vertical number line diagram.

data.title: Describe situations in which opposite quantities combine to make 0
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of operations with fractions to add, subtract, multiply, and divide rational numbers.
data.description: Describe situations in which opposite quantities combine to make 0. For example, a hydrogen atom has 0 charge because its two constituents are oppositely charged.

data.title: Understand p + q as the number located a distance |q| from p
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of operations with fractions to add, subtract, multiply, and divide rational numbers.
data.description: Understand p + q as the number located a distance |q| from p, in the positive or negative direction depending on whether q is positive or negative. Show that a number and its opposite have a sum of 0 (are additive inverses). Interpret sums of rational numbers by describing real-world contexts.

data.title: Understand subtraction of rational numbers as adding the additive inverse
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of operations with fractions to add, subtract, multiply, and divide rational numbers.
data.description: Understand subtraction of rational numbers as adding the additive inverse, p – q = p + (–q). Show that the distance between two rational numbers on the number line is the absolute value of their difference, and apply this principle in real-world contexts.

data.title: Apply properties of operations as strategies to add and subtract rational numbers
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of operations with fractions to add, subtract, multiply, and divide rational numbers.
data.description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to add and subtract rational numbers.

data.title: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication and division to rational numbers
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of operations with fractions to add, subtract, multiply, and divide rational numbers.
data.description: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication and division and of fractions to multiply and divide rational numbers.

data.title: Understand that multiplication is extended from fractions to rational numbers
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of operations with fractions to add, subtract, multiply, and divide rational numbers.
data.description: Understand that multiplication is extended from fractions to rational numbers by requiring that operations continue to satisfy the properties of operations, particularly the distributive property, leading to products such as (–1)(–1) = 1 and the rules for multiplying signed numbers. Interpret products of rational numbers by describing real-world contexts.

data.title: Understand that integers can be divided
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of operations with fractions to add, subtract, multiply, and divide rational numbers.
data.description: Understand that integers can be divided, provided that the divisor is not zero, and every quotient of integers (with non-zero divisor) is a rational number. If p and q are integers, then –(p/q) = (–p)/q = p/(–q). Interpret quotients of rational numbers by describing real-world contexts.

data.title: Apply properties of operations as strategies to multiply and divide rational numbers
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of operations with fractions to add, subtract, multiply, and divide rational numbers.
data.description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to multiply and divide rational numbers.

data.title: Convert a rational number to a decimal using long division
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of operations with fractions to add, subtract, multiply, and divide rational numbers.
data.description: Convert a rational number to a decimal using long division; know that the decimal form of a rational number terminates in 0s or eventually repeats.

data.title: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving the four operations with rational numbers
data.cluster: Apply and extend previous understandings of operations with fractions to add, subtract, multiply, and divide rational numbers.
data.description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving the four operations with rational numbers.

data.title: Compute unit rates associated with ratios of fractions
data.cluster: Analyze proportional relationships and use them to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
data.description: Compute unit rates associated with ratios of fractions, including ratios of lengths, areas and other quantities measured in like or different units. For example, if a person walks 1/2 mile in each 1/4 hour, compute the unit rate as the complex fraction 1/2/1/4 miles per hour, equivalently 2 miles per hour.

data.title: Recognize and represent proportional relationships
data.cluster: Analyze proportional relationships and use them to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
data.description: Recognize and represent proportional relationships between quantities.

data.title: Decide whether two quantities are in a proportional relationship
data.cluster: Analyze proportional relationships and use them to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
data.description: Decide whether two quantities are in a proportional relationship, e.g., by testing for equivalent ratios in a table or graphing on a coordinate plane and observing whether the graph is a straight line through the origin.

data.title: Identify the constant of proportionality (unit rate)
data.cluster: Analyze proportional relationships and use them to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
data.description: Identify the constant of proportionality (unit rate) in tables, graphs, equations, diagrams, and verbal descriptions of proportional relationships.

data.title: Represent proportional relationships by equations
data.cluster: Analyze proportional relationships and use them to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
data.description: Represent proportional relationships by equations. For example, if total cost t is proportional to the number n of items purchased at a constant price p, the relationship between the total cost and the number of items can be expressed as t = pn.

data.title: Explain what a point (x, y) on the graph of a proportional relationship means
data.cluster: Analyze proportional relationships and use them to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
data.description: Explain what a point (x, y) on the graph of a proportional relationship means in terms of the situation, with special attention to the points (0, 0) and (1, r) where r is the unit rate.

data.title: Use proportional relationships to solve multistep ratio and percent problems
data.cluster: Analyze proportional relationships and use them to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
data.description: Use proportional relationships to solve multistep ratio and percent problems. Examples: simple interest, tax, markups and markdowns, gratuities and commissions, fees, percent increase and decrease, percent error.

data.title: Understand that statistics can be used to gain information about a population
data.cluster: Use random sampling to draw inferences about a population.
data.description: Understand that statistics can be used to gain information about a population by examining a sample of the population; generalizations about a population from a sample are valid only if the sample is representative of that population. Understand that random sampling tends to produce representative samples and support valid inferences.

data.title: Use data from a random sample to draw inferences about a population
data.cluster: Use random sampling to draw inferences about a population.
data.description: Use data from a random sample to draw inferences about a population with an unknown characteristic of interest. Generate multiple samples (or simulated samples) of the same size to gauge the variation in estimates or predictions. For example, estimate the mean word length in a book by randomly sampling words from the book; predict the winner of a school election based on randomly sampled survey data. Gauge how far off the estimate or prediction might be.

data.title: Informally assess the degree of visual overlap of two numerical data distributions
data.cluster: Draw informal comparative inferences about two populations.
data.description: Informally assess the degree of visual overlap of two numerical data distributions with similar variabilities, measuring the difference between the centers by expressing it as a multiple of a measure of variability. For example, the mean height of players on the basketball team is 10 cm greater than the mean height of players on the soccer team, about twice the variability (mean absolute deviation) on either team; on a dot plot, the separation between the two distributions of heights is noticeable.

data.title: Use measures of center and measures of variability to draw informal comparative inferences
data.cluster: Draw informal comparative inferences about two populations.
data.description: Use measures of center and measures of variability for numerical data from random samples to draw informal comparative inferences about two populations. For example, decide whether the words in a chapter of a seventh-grade science book are generally longer than the words in a chapter of a fourth-grade science book.

data.title: Understand that the probability of a chance event is a number between 0 and 1
data.cluster: Investigate chance processes and develop, use, and evaluate probability models.
data.description: Understand that the probability of a chance event is a number between 0 and 1 that expresses the likelihood of the event occurring. Larger numbers indicate greater likelihood. A probability near 0 indicates an unlikely event, a probability around 1/2 indicates an event that is neither unlikely nor likely, and a probability near 1 indicates a likely event.

data.title: Approximate the probability of a chance event by collecting data
data.cluster: Investigate chance processes and develop, use, and evaluate probability models.
data.description: Approximate the probability of a chance event by collecting data on the chance process that produces it and observing its long-run relative frequency, and predict the approximate relative frequency given the probability. For example, when rolling a number cube 600 times, predict that a 3 or 6 would be rolled roughly 200 times, but probably not exactly 200 times.

data.title: Develop a probability model and use it to find probabilities of events
data.cluster: Investigate chance processes and develop, use, and evaluate probability models.
data.description: Develop a probability model and use it to find probabilities of events. Compare probabilities from a model to observed frequencies; if the agreement is not good, explain possible sources of the discrepancy.

data.title: Develop a uniform probability model
data.cluster: Investigate chance processes and develop, use, and evaluate probability models.
data.description: Develop a uniform probability model by assigning equal probability to all outcomes, and use the model to determine probabilities of events. For example, if a student is selected at random from a class, find the probability that Jane will be selected and the probability that a girl will be selected.

data.title: Develop a probability model by observing frequencies
data.cluster: Investigate chance processes and develop, use, and evaluate probability models.
data.description: Develop a probability model (which may not be uniform) by observing frequencies in data generated from a chance process. For example, find the approximate probability that a spinning penny will land heads up or that a tossed paper cup will land open-end down. Do the outcomes for the spinning penny appear to be equally likely based on the observed frequencies?

data.title: Find probabilities of compound events
data.cluster: Investigate chance processes and develop, use, and evaluate probability models.
data.description: Find probabilities of compound events using organized lists, tables, tree diagrams, and simulation.

data.title: Understand the probability of a compound event
data.cluster: Investigate chance processes and develop, use, and evaluate probability models.
data.description: Understand that, just as with simple events, the probability of a compound event is the fraction of outcomes in the sample space for which the compound event occurs.

data.title: Represent sample spaces for compound events
data.cluster: Investigate chance processes and develop, use, and evaluate probability models.
data.description: Represent sample spaces for compound events using methods such as organized lists, tables and tree diagrams. For an event described in everyday language (e.g., “rolling double sixes”), identify the outcomes in the sample space which compose the event.

data.title: Design and use a simulation to generate frequencies for compound events
data.cluster: Investigate chance processes and develop, use, and evaluate probability models.
data.description: Design and use a simulation to generate frequencies for compound events. For example, use random digits as a simulation tool to approximate the answer to the question: If 40% of donors have type A blood, what is the probability that it will take at least 4 donors to find one with type A blood?

data.title: Properties of integer exponents
data.cluster: Work with radicals and integer exponents.
data.description: Know and apply the properties of integer exponents to generate equivalent numerical expressions. For example, 3² × 3⁻⁵ = 3⁻³ = 1/3³ = 1/27.

data.title: Square and cube roots
data.cluster: Work with radicals and integer exponents.
data.description: Use square root and cube root symbols to represent solutions to equations of the form x² = p and x³ = p, where p is a positive rational number. Evaluate square roots of small perfect squares and cube roots of small perfect cubes. Know that √2 is irrational.

data.title: Scientific notation estimation
data.cluster: Work with radicals and integer exponents.
data.description: Use numbers expressed in the form of a single digit times an integer power of 10 to estimate very large or very small quantities, and to express how many times as much one is than the other. For example, estimate the population of the United States as 3 × 10⁸ and the population of the world as 7 × 10⁹, and determine that the world population is more than 20 times larger.

data.title: Operations with scientific notation
data.cluster: Work with radicals and integer exponents.
data.description: Perform operations with numbers expressed in scientific notation, including problems where both decimal and scientific notation are used. Use scientific notation and choose units of appropriate size for measurements of very large or very small quantities (e.g., use millimeters per year for seafloor spreading). Interpret scientific notation that has been generated by technology.

data.title: Graph proportional relationships
data.cluster: Understand the connections between proportional relationships, lines, and linear equations.
data.description: Graph proportional relationships, interpreting the unit rate as the slope of the graph. Compare two different proportional relationships represented in different ways. For example, compare a distance-time graph to a distance-time equation to determine which of two moving objects has greater speed.

data.title: Deriving slope and linear equations
data.cluster: Understand the connections between proportional relationships, lines, and linear equations.
data.description: Use similar triangles to explain why the slope m is the same between any two distinct points on a non-vertical line in the coordinate plane; derive the equation y = mx for a line through the origin and the equation y = mx + b for a line intercepting the vertical axis at b.

data.title: Linear equations with one, infinite, or no solutions
data.cluster: Analyze and solve linear equations and pairs of simultaneous linear equations.
data.description: Solve linear equations in one variable. Give examples of linear equations in one variable with one solution, infinitely many solutions, or no solutions. Show which of these possibilities is the case by successively transforming the given equation into simpler forms, until an equivalent equation of the form x = a, a = a, or a = b results (where a and b are different numbers).

data.title: Solving linear equations with rational coefficients
data.cluster: Analyze and solve linear equations and pairs of simultaneous linear equations.
data.description: Solve linear equations in one variable. Solve linear equations with rational number coefficients, including equations whose solutions require expanding expressions using the distributive property and collecting like terms.

data.title: Systems of linear equations and intersections
data.cluster: Analyze and solve pairs of simultaneous linear equations.
data.description: Analyze and solve pairs of simultaneous linear equations. Understand that solutions to a system of two linear equations in two variables correspond to points of intersection of their graphs, because points of intersection satisfy both equations simultaneously.

data.title: Solving systems of linear equations algebraically
data.cluster: Analyze and solve pairs of simultaneous linear equations.
data.description: Analyze and solve pairs of simultaneous linear equations. Solve systems of two linear equations in two variables algebraically, and estimate solutions by graphing the equations. Solve simple cases by inspection. For example, 3x + 2y = 5 and 3x + 2y = 6 have no solution because 3x + 2y cannot simultaneously be 5 and 6.

data.title: Solving real-world problems with systems of equations
data.cluster: Analyze and solve pairs of simultaneous linear equations.
data.description: Analyze and solve pairs of simultaneous linear equations. Solve real-world and mathematical problems leading to two linear equations in two variables. For example, given coordinates for two pairs of points, determine whether the line through the first pair of points intersects the line through the second pair.

data.title: Function definition and graphs
data.description: Understand that a function is a rule that assigns to each input exactly one output. The graph of a function is the set of ordered pairs consisting of an input and the corresponding output.

data.title: Comparing functions
data.description: Compare properties of two functions each represented in a different way (algebraically, graphically, numerically in tables, or by verbal descriptions). For example, given a linear function represented by a table of values and a linear function represented by an algebraic expression, determine which function has the greater rate of change.

data.title: Linear and non-linear functions
data.description: Interpret the equation y = mx + b as defining a linear function, whose graph is a straight line; give examples of functions that are not linear. For example, the function A = s² giving the area of a square as a function of its side length is not linear because its graph contains the points (1,1), (2,4) and (3,9), which are not on a straight line.

data.title: Constructing linear functions
data.cluster: Use functions to model relationships between quantities.
data.description: Construct a function to model a linear relationship between two quantities. Determine the rate of change and initial value of the function from a description of a relationship or from two (x, y) values, including reading these from a table or from a graph. Interpret the rate of change and initial value of a linear function in terms of the situation it models, and in terms of its graph or a table of values.

data.title: Qualitative description of functions
data.cluster: Use functions to model relationships between quantities.
data.description: Describe qualitatively the functional relationship between two quantities by analyzing a graph (e.g., where the function is increasing or decreasing, linear or nonlinear). Sketch a graph that exhibits the qualitative features of a function that has been described verbally.

data.title: Properties of transformations: Lines
data.cluster: Understand congruence and similarity using physical models, transparencies, or geometry software.
data.description: Verify experimentally the properties of rotations, reflections, and translations: Lines are taken to lines, and line segments to line segments of the same length.

data.title: Properties of transformations: Angles
data.cluster: Understand congruence and similarity using physical models, transparencies, or geometry software.
data.description: Verify experimentally the properties of rotations, reflections, and translations: Angles are taken to angles of the same measure.

data.title: Properties of transformations: Parallel lines
data.cluster: Understand congruence and similarity using physical models, transparencies, or geometry software.
data.description: Verify experimentally the properties of rotations, reflections, and translations: Parallel lines are taken to parallel lines.

data.title: Congruence via rigid motions
data.cluster: Understand congruence and similarity using physical models, transparencies, or geometry software.
data.description: Understand that a two-dimensional figure is congruent to another if the second can be obtained from the first by a sequence of rotations, reflections, and translations; given two congruent figures, describe a sequence that exhibits the congruence between them.

data.title: Coordinates and transformations
data.cluster: Understand congruence and similarity using physical models, transparencies, or geometry software.
data.description: Describe the effect of dilations, translations, rotations, and reflections on two-dimensional figures using coordinates.

data.title: Similarity via transformations
data.cluster: Understand congruence and similarity using physical models, transparencies, or geometry software.
data.description: Understand that a two-dimensional figure is similar to another if the second can be obtained from the first by a sequence of rotations, reflections, translations, and dilations; given two similar two-dimensional figures, describe a sequence that exhibits the similarity between them.

data.title: Angle arguments and similarity
data.cluster: Understand congruence and similarity using physical models, transparencies, or geometry software.
data.description: Use informal arguments to establish facts about the angle sum and exterior angle of triangles, about the angles created when parallel lines are cut by a transversal, and the angle-angle criterion for similarity of triangles. For example, arrange three copies of the same triangle so that the sum of the three angles appears to form a line, and give an argument in terms of transversals why this is so.

data.title: Proof of Pythagorean Theorem
data.cluster: Understand and apply the Pythagorean Theorem.
data.description: Explain a proof of the Pythagorean Theorem and its converse.

data.title: Applying Pythagorean Theorem
data.cluster: Understand and apply the Pythagorean Theorem.
data.description: Apply the Pythagorean Theorem to determine unknown side lengths in right triangles in real-world and mathematical problems in two and three dimensions.

data.title: Distance in coordinate system
data.cluster: Understand and apply the Pythagorean Theorem.
data.description: Apply the Pythagorean Theorem to find the distance between two points in a coordinate system.

data.title: Volumes of cones, cylinders, and spheres
data.cluster: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving volume of cylinders, cones, and spheres.
data.description: Know the formulas for the volumes of cones, cylinders, and spheres and use them to solve real-world and mathematical problems.

data.title: Know irrational numbers and decimal expansions
data.cluster: Know that there are numbers that are not rational, and approximate them by rational numbers.
data.description: Know that numbers that are not rational are called irrational. Understand informally that every number has a decimal expansion; for rational numbers show that the decimal expansion repeats eventually, and convert a decimal expansion which repeats eventually into a rational number.

data.title: Approximate irrational numbers
data.cluster: Know that there are numbers that are not rational, and approximate them by rational numbers.
data.description: Use rational approximations of irrational numbers to compare the size of irrational numbers, locate them approximately on a number line diagram, and estimate the value of expressions (e.g., π²). For example, by truncating the decimal expansion of √2, show that √2 is between 1 and 2, then between 1.4 and 1.5, and explain how to continue on to get better approximations.

data.title: Scatter plots and association
data.cluster: Investigate patterns of association in bivariate data.
data.description: Construct and interpret scatter plots for bivariate measurement data to investigate patterns of association between two quantities. Describe patterns such as clustering, outliers, positive or negative association, linear association, and nonlinear association.

data.title: Linear models for data
data.cluster: Investigate patterns of association in bivariate data.
data.description: Know that straight lines are widely used to model relationships between two quantitative variables. For scatter plots that suggest a linear association, informally fit a straight line, and informally assess the model fit by judging the closeness of the data points to the line.

data.title: Interpreting linear models
data.cluster: Investigate patterns of association in bivariate data.
data.description: Use the equation of a linear model to solve problems in the context of bivariate measurement data, interpreting the slope and intercept. For example, in a linear model for a biology experiment, interpret a slope of 1.5 cm/hr as meaning that an additional hour of sunlight each day is associated with an additional 1.5 cm in mature plant height.

data.title: Bivariate categorical data
data.cluster: Investigate patterns of association in bivariate data.
data.description: Understand that patterns of association can also be seen in bivariate categorical data by displaying frequencies and relative frequencies in a two-way table. Construct and interpret a two-way table summarizing data on two categorical variables collected from the same subjects. Use relative frequencies calculated for rows or columns to describe possible association between the two variables. For example, collect data from students in your class on whether or not they have a curfew on school nights and whether or not they have assigned chores at home. Is there evidence that those who have a curfew also tend to have chores?

data.title: A-APR.1
data.description: Understand that polynomials form a system analogous to the integers, namely, they are closed under the operations of addition, subtraction, and multiplication; add, subtract, and multiply polynomials.

data.title: A-APR.2
data.cluster: Understand the relationship between zeros and factors of polynomials
data.description: Know and apply the Remainder Theorem: For a polynomial p(x) and a number a, the remainder on division by x – a is p(a), so p(a) = 0 if and only if (x – a) is a factor of p(x).

data.title: A-APR.3
data.cluster: Understand the relationship between zeros and factors of polynomials
data.description: Identify zeros of polynomials when suitable factorizations are available, and use the zeros to construct a rough graph of the function defined by the polynomial.

data.title: A-APR.4
data.cluster: Use polynomial identities to solve problems
data.description: Prove polynomial identities and use them to describe numerical relationships. For example, the polynomial identity (x2 + y2)2 = (x2 – y2)2 + (2xy)2 can be used to generate Pythagorean triples.

data.title: A-APR.5
data.cluster: Use polynomial identities to solve problems
data.description: (+) Know and apply the Binomial Theorem for the expansion of (x + y)n in powers of x and y for a positive integer n, where x and y are any numbers, with coefficients determined for example by Pascal’s Triangle.

data.title: A-APR.6
data.description: Rewrite simple rational expressions in different forms; write a(x)/b(x) in the form q(x) + r(x)/b(x), where a(x), b(x), q(x), and r(x) are polynomials with the degree of r(x) less than the degree of b(x), using inspection, long division, or, for the more complicated examples, a computer algebra system.

data.title: A-APR.7
data.description: (+) Understand that rational expressions form a system analogous to the rational numbers, closed under addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division by a nonzero rational expression; add, subtract, multiply, and divide rational expressions.

data.title: A-CED.1
data.cluster: Create equations that describe numbers or relationships
data.description: Create equations and inequalities in one variable and use them to solve problems. Include equations arising from linear and quadratic functions, and simple rational and exponential functions.

data.title: A-CED.2
data.cluster: Create equations that describe numbers or relationships
data.description: Create equations in two or more variables to represent relationships between quantities; graph equations on coordinate axes with labels and scales.

data.title: A-CED.3
data.cluster: Create equations that describe numbers or relationships
data.description: Represent constraints by equations or inequalities, and by systems of equations and/or inequalities, and interpret solutions as viable or non-viable options in a modeling context. For example, represent inequalities describing nutritional and cost constraints on combinations of different foods.

data.title: A-CED.4
data.cluster: Create equations that describe numbers or relationships
data.description: Rearrange formulas to highlight a quantity of interest, using the same reasoning as in solving equations. For example, rearrange Ohm’s law V = IR to highlight resistance R.

data.title: A-REI.1
data.cluster: Understand solving equations as a process of reasoning and explain the reasoning
data.description: Explain each step in solving a simple equation as following from the equality of numbers asserted at the previous step, starting from the assumption that the original equation has a solution. Construct a viable argument to justify a solution method.

data.title: A-REI.10
data.cluster: Represent and solve equations and inequalities graphically
data.description: Understand that the graph of an equation in two variables is the set of all its solutions plotted in the coordinate plane, often forming a curve (which could be a line).

data.title: A-REI.11
data.cluster: Represent and solve equations and inequalities graphically
data.description: Explain why the x-coordinates of the points where the graphs of the equations y = f(x) and y = g(x) intersect are the solutions of the equation f(x) = g(x); find the solutions approximately, e.g., using technology to graph the functions, make tables of values, or find successive approximations. Include cases where f(x) and/or g(x) are linear, polynomial, rational, absolute value, exponential, and logarithmic functions.★

data.title: A-REI.12
data.cluster: Represent and solve equations and inequalities graphically
data.description: Graph the solutions to a linear inequality in two variables as a half-plane (excluding the boundary in the case of a strict inequality), and graph the solution set to a system of linear inequalities in two variables as the intersection of the corresponding half-planes.

data.title: A-REI.2
data.cluster: Understand solving equations as a process of reasoning and explain the reasoning
data.description: Solve simple rational and radical equations in one variable, and give examples showing how extraneous solutions may arise.

data.title: A-REI.3
data.cluster: Solve equations and inequalities in one variable
data.description: Solve linear equations and inequalities in one variable, including equations with coefficients represented by letters.

data.title: A-REI.4
data.cluster: Solve equations and inequalities in one variable
data.description: Solve quadratic equations in one variable.

data.title: A-REI.4.a
data.cluster: Solve equations and inequalities in one variable
data.description: Use the method of completing the square to transform any quadratic equation in x into an equation of the form (x – p)2 = q that has the same solutions. Derive the quadratic formula from this form.

data.title: A-REI.4.b
data.cluster: Solve equations and inequalities in one variable
data.description: Solve quadratic equations by inspection (e.g., for x2 = 49), taking square roots, completing the square, the quadratic formula and factoring, as appropriate to the initial form of the equation. Recognize when the quadratic formula gives complex solutions and write them as a ± bi for real numbers a and b.

data.title: A-REI.5
data.description: Prove that, given a system of two equations in two variables, replacing one equation by the sum of that equation and a multiple of the other produces a system with the same solutions.

data.title: A-REI.6
data.description: Solve systems of linear equations exactly and approximately (e.g., with graphs), focusing on pairs of linear equations in two variables.

data.title: A-REI.7
data.description: Solve a simple system consisting of a linear equation and a quadratic equation in two variables algebraically and graphically. For example, find the points of intersection between the line y = –3x and the circle x2 + y2 = 3.

data.title: A-REI.8
data.description: (+) Represent a system of linear equations as a single matrix equation in a vector variable.

data.title: A-REI.9
data.description: (+) Find the inverse of a matrix if it exists and use it to solve systems of linear equations (using technology for matrices of dimension 3 × 3 or greater).

data.title: A-SSE.1
data.description: Interpret expressions that represent a quantity in terms of its context.★

data.title: A-SSE.1.a
data.description: Interpret parts of an expression, such as terms, factors, and coefficients.

data.title: A-SSE.1.b
data.description: Interpret complicated expressions by viewing one or more of their parts as a single entity. For example, interpret P(1+r)n as the product of P and a factor not depending on P.

data.title: A-SSE.2
data.description: Use the structure of an expression to identify ways to rewrite it. For example, see x4 – y4 as (x2)2 – (y2)2, thus recognizing it as a difference of squares that can be factored as (x2 – y2)(x2 + y2).

data.title: A-SSE.3
data.cluster: Write expressions in equivalent forms to solve problems
data.description: Choose and produce an equivalent form of an expression to reveal and explain properties of the quantity represented by the expression.★

data.title: A-SSE.3.a
data.cluster: Write expressions in equivalent forms to solve problems
data.description: Factor a quadratic expression to reveal the zeros of the function it defines.

data.title: A-SSE.3.b
data.cluster: Write expressions in equivalent forms to solve problems
data.description: Complete the square in a quadratic expression to reveal the maximum or minimum value of the function it defines.

data.title: A-SSE.3.c
data.cluster: Write expressions in equivalent forms to solve problems
data.description: Use the properties of exponents to transform expressions for exponential functions. For example the expression 1.15t can be rewritten as (1.151/12)12t ≈ 1.01212t to reveal the approximate equivalent monthly interest rate if the annual rate is 15%.

data.title: A-SSE.4
data.cluster: Write expressions in equivalent forms to solve problems
data.description: Derive the formula for the sum of a finite geometric series (when the common ratio is not 1), and use the formula to solve problems. For example, calculate mortgage payments.★

data.title: Determine Explicit Expression or Recursive Process
data.cluster: Build a function that models a relationship between two quantities
data.description: Determine an explicit expression, a recursive process, or steps for calculation from a context.

data.title: Combine Standard Function Types
data.cluster: Build a function that models a relationship between two quantities
data.description: Combine standard function types using arithmetic operations. For example, build a function that models the temperature of a cooling body by adding a constant function to a decaying exponential, and relate these functions to the model.

data.title: Compose Functions
data.cluster: Build a function that models a relationship between two quantities
data.description: (+) Compose functions. For example, if T(y) is the temperature in the atmosphere as a function of height, and h(t) is the height of a weather balloon as a function of time, then T(h(t)) is the temperature at the location of the weather balloon as a function of time.

data.title: Write Arithmetic and Geometric Sequences
data.cluster: Build a function that models a relationship between two quantities
data.description: Write arithmetic and geometric sequences both recursively and with an explicit formula, use them to model situations, and translate between the two forms.★

data.title: Identify Effects of Transformations on Graphs
data.cluster: Build new functions from existing functions
data.description: Identify the effect on the graph of replacing f(x) by f(x) + k, k f(x), f(kx), and f(x + k) for specific values of k (both positive and negative); find the value of k given the graphs. Experiment with cases and illustrate an explanation of the effects on the graph using technology. Include recognizing even and odd functions from their graphs and algebraic expressions for them.

data.title: Solve for Inverse Function
data.cluster: Build new functions from existing functions
data.description: Solve an equation of the form f(x) = c for a simple function f that has an inverse and write an expression for the inverse. For example, f(x) =2 x³ or f(x) = (x+1)/(x–1) for x ≠ 1.

data.title: Verify Inverse Functions by Composition
data.cluster: Build new functions from existing functions
data.description: (+) Verify by composition that one function is the inverse of another.

data.title: Read Inverse Values from Graph or Table
data.cluster: Build new functions from existing functions
data.description: (+) Read values of an inverse function from a graph or a table, given that the function has an inverse.

data.title: Produce Invertible Function by Restricting Domain
data.cluster: Build new functions from existing functions
data.description: (+) Produce an invertible function from a non-invertible function by restricting the domain.

data.title: Understand Inverse Relationship of Exponents and Logarithms
data.cluster: Build new functions from existing functions
data.description: (+) Understand the inverse relationship between exponents and logarithms and use this relationship to solve problems involving logarithms and exponents.

data.title: Understand Function Definition and Graph
data.cluster: Understand the concept of a function and use function notation
data.description: Understand that a function from one set (called the domain) to another set (called the range) assigns to each element of the domain exactly one element of the range. If f is a function and x is an element of its domain, then f(x) denotes the output of f corresponding to the input x. The graph of f is the graph of the equation y = f(x).

data.title: Use Function Notation
data.cluster: Understand the concept of a function and use function notation
data.description: Use function notation, evaluate functions for inputs in their domains, and interpret statements that use function notation in terms of a context.

data.title: Sequences as Functions
data.cluster: Understand the concept of a function and use function notation
data.description: Recognize that sequences are functions, sometimes defined recursively, whose domain is a subset of the integers. For example, the Fibonacci sequence is defined recursively by f(0) = f(1) = 1, f(n+1) = f(n) + f(n-1) for n ≥ 1.

data.title: Interpret Key Features of Functions
data.cluster: Interpret functions that arise in applications in terms of the context
data.description: For a function that models a relationship between two quantities, interpret key features of graphs and tables in terms of the quantities, and sketch graphs showing key features given a verbal description of the relationship. Key features include: intercepts; intervals where the function is increasing, decreasing, positive, or negative; relative maximums and minimums; symmetries; end behavior; and periodicity.★

data.title: Relate Domain to Context
data.cluster: Interpret functions that arise in applications in terms of the context
data.description: Relate the domain of a function to its graph and, where applicable, to the quantitative relationship it describes. For example, if the function h(n) gives the number of person-hours it takes to assemble n engines in a factory, then the positive integers would be an appropriate domain for the function.★

data.title: Calculate Average Rate of Change
data.cluster: Interpret functions that arise in applications in terms of the context
data.description: Calculate and interpret the average rate of change of a function (presented symbolically or as a table) over a specified interval. Estimate the rate of change from a graph.★

data.title: Graph Linear and Quadratic Functions
data.description: Graph linear and quadratic functions and show intercepts, maxima, and minima.

data.title: Graph Square Root, Cube Root, and Piecewise Functions
data.description: Graph square root, cube root, and piecewise-defined functions, including step functions and absolute value functions.

data.title: Graph Polynomial Functions
data.description: Graph polynomial functions, identifying zeros when suitable factorizations are available, and showing end behavior.

data.title: Graph Rational Functions
data.description: (+) Graph rational functions, identifying zeros and asymptotes when suitable factorizations are available, and showing end behavior.

data.title: Graph Exponential, Logarithmic, and Trigonometric Functions
data.description: Graph exponential and logarithmic functions, showing intercepts and end behavior, and trigonometric functions, showing period, midline, and amplitude.

data.title: Factor and Complete the Square in Quadratic Functions
data.description: Use the process of factoring and completing the square in a quadratic function to show zeros, extreme values, and symmetry of the graph, and interpret these in terms of a context.

data.title: Interpret Expressions for Exponential Functions
data.description: Use the properties of exponents to interpret expressions for exponential functions. For example, identify percent rate of change in functions such as y = (1.02)ᵗ, y = (0.97)ᵗ, y = (1.01)¹²ᵗ, y = (1.2)ᵗ/¹⁰, and classify them as representing exponential growth or decay.

data.title: Compare Functions Represented Differently
data.description: Compare properties of two functions each represented in a different way (algebraically, graphically, numerically in tables, or by verbal descriptions). For example, given a graph of one quadratic function and an algebraic expression for another, say which has the larger maximum.

data.title: Prove Growth of Linear and Exponential Functions
data.cluster: Construct and compare linear, quadratic, and exponential models and solve problems
data.description: Prove that linear functions grow by equal differences over equal intervals, and that exponential functions grow by equal factors over equal intervals.

data.title: Recognize Constant Rate of Change
data.cluster: Construct and compare linear, quadratic, and exponential models and solve problems
data.description: Recognize situations in which one quantity changes at a constant rate per unit interval relative to another.

data.title: Recognize Constant Percent Rate
data.cluster: Construct and compare linear, quadratic, and exponential models and solve problems
data.description: Recognize situations in which a quantity grows or decays by a constant percent rate per unit interval relative to another.

data.title: Construct Linear and Exponential Functions
data.cluster: Construct and compare linear, quadratic, and exponential models and solve problems
data.description: Construct linear and exponential functions, including arithmetic and geometric sequences, given a graph, a description of a relationship, or two input-output pairs (include reading these from a table).

data.title: Compare Growth of Exponential and Polynomial Functions
data.cluster: Construct and compare linear, quadratic, and exponential models and solve problems
data.description: Observe using graphs and tables that a quantity increasing exponentially eventually exceeds a quantity increasing linearly, quadratically, or (more generally) as a polynomial function.

data.title: Express Exponential Models as Logarithms
data.cluster: Construct and compare linear, quadratic, and exponential models and solve problems
data.description: For exponential models, express as a logarithm the solution to abᶜᵗ = d where a, c, and d are numbers and the base b is 2, 10, or e; evaluate the logarithm using technology.

data.title: Interpret Parameters in Linear or Exponential Functions
data.cluster: Interpret expressions for functions in terms of the situation they model
data.description: Interpret the parameters in a linear or exponential function in terms of a context.

data.title: Understand Radian Measure
data.cluster: Extend the domain of trigonometric functions using the unit circle
data.description: Understand radian measure of an angle as the length of the arc on the unit circle subtended by the angle.

data.title: Extend Trigonometric Functions Using Unit Circle
data.cluster: Extend the domain of trigonometric functions using the unit circle
data.description: Explain how the unit circle in the coordinate plane enables the extension of trigonometric functions to all real numbers, interpreted as radian measures of angles traversed counterclockwise around the unit circle.

data.title: Use Special Triangles and Unit Circle
data.cluster: Extend the domain of trigonometric functions using the unit circle
data.description: (+) Use special triangles to determine geometrically the values of sine, cosine, tangent for π/3, π/4 and π/6, and use the unit circle to express the values of sine, cosine, and tangent for π–x, π+x, and 2π–x in terms of their values for x, where x is any real number.

data.title: Explain Symmetry and Periodicity
data.cluster: Extend the domain of trigonometric functions using the unit circle
data.description: (+) Use the unit circle to explain symmetry (odd and even) and periodicity of trigonometric functions.

data.title: Model Periodic Phenomena
data.cluster: Model periodic phenomena with trigonometric functions
data.description: Choose trigonometric functions to model periodic phenomena with specified amplitude, frequency, and midline.★

data.title: Restrict Domain to Construct Inverse
data.cluster: Model periodic phenomena with trigonometric functions
data.description: (+) Understand that restricting a trigonometric function to a domain on which it is always increasing or always decreasing allows its inverse to be constructed.

data.title: Use Inverse Functions to Solve Equations
data.cluster: Model periodic phenomena with trigonometric functions
data.description: (+) Use inverse functions to solve trigonometric equations that arise in modeling contexts; evaluate the solutions using technology, and interpret them in terms of the context.★

data.title: Prove and Use Pythagorean Identity
data.description: Prove the Pythagorean identity sin²(θ) + cos²(θ) = 1 and use it to find sin(θ), cos(θ), or tan(θ) given sin(θ), cos(θ), or tan(θ) and the quadrant of the angle.

data.title: Prove Addition and Subtraction Formulas
data.description: (+) Prove the addition and subtraction formulas for sine, cosine, and tangent and use them to solve problems.

data.title: Similarity of Circles
data.cluster: Understand and apply theorems about circles
data.description: Prove that all circles are similar.

data.title: Relationships Among Angles, Radii, and Chords
data.cluster: Understand and apply theorems about circles
data.description: Identify and describe relationships among inscribed angles, radii, and chords. Include the relationship between central, inscribed, and circumscribed angles; inscribed angles on a diameter are right angles; the radius of a circle is perpendicular to the tangent where the radius intersects the circle.

data.title: Inscribed and Circumscribed Circles
data.cluster: Understand and apply theorems about circles
data.description: Construct the inscribed and circumscribed circles of a triangle, and prove properties of angles for a quadrilateral inscribed in a circle.

data.title: Construct Tangent Line
data.cluster: Understand and apply theorems about circles
data.description: (+) Construct a tangent line from a point outside a given circle to the circle.

data.title: Arc Length and Area of Sectors
data.cluster: Find arc lengths and areas of sectors of circles
data.description: Derive using similarity the fact that the length of the arc intercepted by an angle is proportional to the radius, and define the radian measure of the angle as the constant of proportionality; derive the formula for the area of a sector.

data.title: Know Precise Definitions of Geometric Terms
data.cluster: Experiment with transformations in the plane
data.description: Know precise definitions of angle, circle, perpendicular line, parallel line, and line segment, based on the undefined notions of point, line, distance along a line, and distance around a circular arc.

data.title: Represent and Compare Transformations
data.cluster: Experiment with transformations in the plane
data.description: Represent transformations in the plane using, e.g., transparencies and geometry software; describe transformations as functions that take points in the plane as inputs and give other points as outputs. Compare transformations that preserve distance and angle to those that do not (e.g., translation versus horizontal stretch).

data.title: Symmetries of Basic Polygons
data.cluster: Experiment with transformations in the plane
data.description: Given a rectangle, parallelogram, trapezoid, or regular polygon, describe the rotations and reflections that carry it onto itself.

data.title: Develop Definitions of Rigid Motions
data.cluster: Experiment with transformations in the plane
data.description: Develop definitions of rotations, reflections, and translations in terms of angles, circles, perpendicular lines, parallel lines, and line segments.

data.title: Draw Transformed Figures
data.cluster: Experiment with transformations in the plane
data.description: Given a geometric figure and a rotation, reflection, or translation, draw the transformed figure using, e.g., graph paper, tracing paper, or geometry software. Specify a sequence of transformations that will carry a given figure onto another.

data.title: Rigid Motions and Congruence
data.cluster: Understand congruence in terms of rigid motions
data.description: Use geometric descriptions of rigid motions to transform figures and to predict the effect of a given rigid motion on a given figure; given two figures, use the definition of congruence in terms of rigid motions to decide if they are congruent.

data.title: Triangle Congruence and Rigid Motions
data.cluster: Understand congruence in terms of rigid motions
data.description: Use the definition of congruence in terms of rigid motions to show that two triangles are congruent if and only if corresponding pairs of sides and corresponding pairs of angles are congruent.

data.title: Explain Triangle Congruence Criteria
data.cluster: Understand congruence in terms of rigid motions
data.description: Explain how the criteria for triangle congruence (ASA, SAS, and SSS) follow from the definition of congruence in terms of rigid motions.

data.title: Prove Theorems About Triangles
data.description: Prove theorems about triangles. Theorems include: measures of interior angles of a triangle sum to 180°; base angles of isosceles triangles are congruent; the segment joining midpoints of two sides of a triangle is parallel to the third side and half the length; the medians of a triangle meet at a point.

data.title: Prove Theorems About Parallelograms
data.description: Prove theorems about parallelograms. Theorems include: opposite sides are congruent, opposite angles are congruent, the diagonals of a parallelogram bisect each other, and conversely, rectangles are parallelograms with congruent diagonals.

data.title: Prove Theorems About Lines and Angles
data.description: Prove theorems about lines and angles. Theorems include: vertical angles are congruent; when a transversal crosses parallel lines, alternate interior angles are congruent and corresponding angles are congruent; points on a perpendicular bisector of a line segment are exactly those equidistant from the segment’s endpoints.

data.title: Make Formal Geometric Constructions
data.description: Make formal geometric constructions with a variety of tools and methods (compass and straightedge, string, reflective devices, paper folding, dynamic geometric software, etc.). Copying a segment; copying an angle; bisecting a segment; bisecting an angle; constructing perpendicular lines, including the perpendicular bisector of a line segment; and constructing a line parallel to a given line through a point not on the line.

data.title: Construct Inscribed Polygons
data.description: Construct an equilateral triangle, a square, and a regular hexagon inscribed in a circle.

data.title: Arguments for Area and Volume Formulas
data.cluster: Explain volume formulas and use them to solve problems
data.description: Give an informal argument for the formulas for the circumference of a circle, area of a circle, volume of a cylinder, pyramid, and cone. Use dissection arguments, Cavalieri’s principle, and informal limit arguments.

data.title: Volume Formulas for Sphere and Other Solids
data.cluster: Explain volume formulas and use them to solve problems
data.description: (+) Give an informal argument using Cavalieri’s principle for the formulas for the volume of a sphere and other solid figures.

data.title: Use Volume Formulas
data.cluster: Explain volume formulas and use them to solve problems
data.description: Use volume formulas for cylinders, pyramids, cones, and spheres to solve problems.

data.title: Cross-Sections and Rotations
data.cluster: Visualize relationships between two-dimensional and three-dimensional objects
data.description: Identify the shapes of two-dimensional cross-sections of three-dimensional objects, and identify three-dimensional objects generated by rotations of two-dimensional objects.

data.title: Derive Equation of a Circle
data.cluster: Translate between the geometric description and the equation for a conic section
data.description: Derive the equation of a circle of given center and radius using the Pythagorean Theorem; complete the square to find the center and radius of a circle given by an equation.

data.title: Derive Equation of a Parabola
data.cluster: Translate between the geometric description and the equation for a conic section
data.description: Derive the equation of a parabola given a focus and directrix.

data.title: Derive Equations of Ellipses and Hyperbolas
data.cluster: Translate between the geometric description and the equation for a conic section
data.description: (+) Derive the equations of ellipses and hyperbolas given the foci, using the fact that the sum or difference of distances from the foci is constant.

data.title: Prove Geometric Theorems Algebraically
data.cluster: Use coordinates to prove simple geometric theorems algebraically
data.description: Use coordinates to prove simple geometric theorems algebraically. For example, prove or disprove that a figure defined by four given points in the coordinate plane is a rectangle; prove or disprove that the point (1, √3) lies on the circle centered at the origin and containing the point (0, 2).

data.title: Slope Criteria for Parallel and Perpendicular Lines
data.cluster: Use coordinates to prove simple geometric theorems algebraically
data.description: Prove the slope criteria for parallel and perpendicular lines and use them to solve geometric problems (e.g., find the equation of a line parallel or perpendicular to a given line that passes through a given point).

data.title: Partition a Line Segment
data.cluster: Use coordinates to prove simple geometric theorems algebraically
data.description: Find the point on a directed line segment between two given points that partitions the segment in a given ratio.

data.title: Compute Perimeters and Areas Using Coordinates
data.cluster: Use coordinates to prove simple geometric theorems algebraically
data.description: Use coordinates to compute perimeters of polygons and areas of triangles and rectangles, e.g., using the distance formula.

data.title: Use Geometric Shapes to Describe Objects
data.cluster: Apply geometric concepts in modeling situations
data.description: Use geometric shapes, their measures, and their properties to describe objects (e.g., modeling a tree trunk or a human torso as a cylinder).

data.title: Apply Concepts of Density
data.cluster: Apply geometric concepts in modeling situations
data.description: Apply concepts of density based on area and volume in modeling situations (e.g., persons per square mile, BTUs per cubic foot).

data.title: Solve Design Problems
data.cluster: Apply geometric concepts in modeling situations
data.description: Apply geometric methods to solve design problems (e.g., designing an object or structure to satisfy physical constraints or minimize cost; working with typographic grid systems based on ratios).

data.title: Verify Properties of Dilations
data.cluster: Understand similarity in terms of similarity transformations
data.description: Verify experimentally the properties of dilations given by a center and a scale factor.

data.title: Dilations and Lines
data.cluster: Understand similarity in terms of similarity transformations
data.description: A dilation takes a line not passing through the center of the dilation to a parallel line, and leaves a line passing through the center unchanged.

data.title: Dilations and Line Segments
data.cluster: Understand similarity in terms of similarity transformations
data.description: The dilation of a line segment is longer or shorter in the ratio given by the scale factor.

data.title: Similarity Transformations and Triangle Similarity
data.cluster: Understand similarity in terms of similarity transformations
data.description: Given two figures, use the definition of similarity in terms of similarity transformations to decide if they are similar; explain using similarity transformations the meaning of similarity for triangles as the equality of all corresponding pairs of angles and the proportionality of all corresponding pairs of sides.

data.title: Establish AA Similarity Criterion
data.cluster: Understand similarity in terms of similarity transformations
data.description: Use the properties of similarity transformations to establish the AA criterion for two triangles to be similar.

data.title: Prove Theorems About Triangles Involving Similarity
data.description: Prove theorems about triangles. Theorems include: a line parallel to one side of a triangle divides the other two proportionally, and conversely; the Pythagorean Theorem proved using triangle similarity.

data.title: Use Congruence and Similarity Criteria
data.description: Use congruence and similarity criteria for triangles to solve problems and to prove relationships in geometric figures.

data.title: Define Trigonometric Ratios
data.cluster: Define trigonometric ratios and solve problems involving right triangles
data.description: Understand that by similarity, side ratios in right triangles are properties of the angles in the triangle, leading to definitions of trigonometric ratios for acute angles.

data.title: Sine and Cosine of Complementary Angles
data.cluster: Define trigonometric ratios and solve problems involving right triangles
data.description: Explain and use the relationship between the sine and cosine of complementary angles.
