data.title: Solve Right Triangles
data.cluster: Define trigonometric ratios and solve problems involving right triangles
data.description: Use trigonometric ratios and the Pythagorean Theorem to solve right triangles in applied problems.

data.title: Prove Laws of Sines and Cosines
data.description: (+) Prove the Laws of Sines and Cosines and use them to solve problems.

data.title: Apply Laws of Sines and Cosines
data.description: (+) Understand and apply the Law of Sines and the Law of Cosines to find unknown measurements in right and non-right triangles (e.g., surveying problems, resultant forces).

data.title: Derive Area Formula for Triangle
data.description: (+) Derive the formula A = 1/2 ab sin(C) for the area of a triangle by drawing an auxiliary line from a vertex perpendicular to the opposite side.

data.title: Count to 100 by Ones and Tens
data.cluster: Know number names and the count sequence.
data.description: Count to 100 by ones and by tens.

data.title: Count Forward From a Given Number
data.cluster: Know number names and the count sequence.
data.description: Count forward beginning from a given number within the known sequence (instead of having to begin at 1).

data.title: Write Numbers 0 to 20
data.cluster: Know number names and the count sequence.
data.description: Write numbers from 0 to 20. Represent a number of objects with a written numeral 0-20 (with 0 representing a count of no objects).

data.title: Relationship Between Numbers and Quantities
data.cluster: Count to tell the number of objects.
data.description: Understand the relationship between numbers and quantities; connect counting to cardinality.

data.title: One-to-One Correspondence
data.cluster: Count to tell the number of objects.
data.description: When counting objects, say the number names in the standard order, pairing each object with one and only one number name and each number name with one and only one object.

data.title: Last Number Represents Quantity
data.cluster: Count to tell the number of objects.
data.description: Understand that the last number name said tells the number of objects counted. The number of objects is the same regardless of their arrangement or the order in which they were counted.

data.title: Successive Numbers Increase Quantity
data.cluster: Count to tell the number of objects.
data.description: Understand that each successive number name refers to a quantity that is one larger.

data.title: Count to Answer How Many
data.cluster: Count to tell the number of objects.
data.description: Count to answer “how many?” questions about as many as 20 things arranged in a line, a rectangular array, or a circle, or as many as 10 things in a scattered configuration; given a number from 1–20, count out that many objects.

data.title: Compare Groups of Objects
data.description: Identify whether the number of objects in one group is greater than, less than, or equal to the number of objects in another group, e.g., by using matching and counting strategies.

data.title: Compare Written Numerals
data.description: Compare two numbers between 1 and 10 presented as written numerals.

data.title: Describe Objects Using Shape Names and Position
data.cluster: Identify and describe shapes (squares, circles, triangles, rectangles, hexagons, cubes, cones, cylinders, and spheres).
data.description: Describe objects in the environment using names of shapes, and describe the relative positions of these objects using terms such as above, below, beside, in front of, behind, and next to.

data.title: Name Shapes Correctly
data.cluster: Identify and describe shapes (squares, circles, triangles, rectangles, hexagons, cubes, cones, cylinders, and spheres).
data.description: Correctly name shapes regardless of their orientations or overall size.

data.title: Identify Two-Dimensional and Three-Dimensional Shapes
data.cluster: Identify and describe shapes (squares, circles, triangles, rectangles, hexagons, cubes, cones, cylinders, and spheres).
data.description: Identify shapes as two-dimensional (lying in a plane, “flat”) or three-dimensional (“solid”).

data.title: Analyze and Compare Shapes
data.cluster: Analyze, compare, create, and compose shapes.
data.description: Analyze and compare two- and three-dimensional shapes, in different sizes and orientations, using informal language to describe their similarities, differences, parts (e.g., number of sides and vertices/“corners”) and other attributes (e.g., having sides of equal length).

data.title: Model Shapes in the World
data.cluster: Analyze, compare, create, and compose shapes.
data.description: Model shapes in the world by building shapes from components (e.g., sticks and clay balls) and drawing shapes.

data.title: Compose Simple Shapes
data.cluster: Analyze, compare, create, and compose shapes.
data.description: Compose simple shapes to form larger shapes. For example, “Can you join these two triangles with full sides touching to make a rectangle?”

data.title: Describe Measurable Attributes
data.description: Describe measurable attributes of objects, such as length or weight. Describe several measurable attributes of a single object.

data.title: Compare Objects with Measurable Attributes
data.description: Directly compare two objects with a measurable attribute in common, to see which object has “more of”/“less of” the attribute, and describe the difference. For example, directly compare the heights of two children and describe one child as taller/shorter.

data.title: Classify and Count Objects
data.cluster: Classify objects and count the number of objects in each category.
data.description: Classify objects into given categories; count the numbers of objects in each category and sort the categories by count.

data.title: Compose and Decompose Numbers 11-19
data.domain: Number and Operations in Base Ten
data.cluster: Work with numbers 11–19 to gain foundations for place value.
data.description: Compose and decompose numbers from 11 to 19 into ten ones and some further ones, e.g., by using objects or drawings, and record each composition or decomposition by a drawing or equation (e.g., 18 = 10 + 8); understand that these numbers are composed of ten ones and one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine ones.

data.title: Represent Addition and Subtraction
data.cluster: Understand addition as putting together and adding to, and understand subtraction as taking apart and taking from.
data.description: Represent addition and subtraction with objects, fingers, mental images, drawings, sounds (e.g., claps), acting out situations, verbal explanations, expressions, or equations.

data.title: Solve Addition and Subtraction Word Problems
data.cluster: Understand addition as putting together and adding to, and understand subtraction as taking apart and taking from.
data.description: Solve addition and subtraction word problems, and add and subtract within 10, e.g., by using objects or drawings to represent the problem.

data.title: Decompose Numbers Less Than or Equal to 10
data.cluster: Understand addition as putting together and adding to, and understand subtraction as taking apart and taking from.
data.description: Decompose numbers less than or equal to 10 into pairs in more than one way, e.g., by using objects or drawings, and record each decomposition by a drawing or equation (e.g., 5 = 2 + 3 and 5 = 4 + 1).

data.title: Make 10
data.cluster: Understand addition as putting together and adding to, and understand subtraction as taking apart and taking from.
data.description: For any number from 1 to 9, find the number that makes 10 when added to the given number, e.g., by using objects or drawings, and record the answer with a drawing or equation.

data.title: Fluently Add and Subtract Within 5
data.cluster: Understand addition as putting together and adding to, and understand subtraction as taking apart and taking from.
data.description: Fluently add and subtract within 5.

data.title: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them
data.description: Mathematically proficient students start by explaining to themselves the meaning of a problem and looking for entry points to its solution. They analyze givens, constraints, relationships, and goals. They make conjectures about the form and meaning of the solution and plan a solution pathway rather than simply jumping into a solution attempt. They consider analogous problems, and try special cases and simpler forms of the original problem in order to gain insight into its solution. They monitor and evaluate their progress and change course if necessary. Older students might, depending on the context of the problem, transform algebraic expressions or change the viewing window on their graphing calculator to get the information they need. Mathematically proficient students can explain correspondences between equations, verbal descriptions, tables, and graphs or draw diagrams of important features and relationships, graph data, and search for regularity or trends. Younger students might rely on using concrete objects or pictures to help conceptualize and solve a problem. Mathematically proficient students check their answers to problems using a different method, and they continually ask themselves, “Does this make sense?” They can understand the approaches of others to solving complex problems and identify correspondences between different approaches.

data.title: Reason abstractly and quantitatively
data.description: Mathematically proficient students make sense of quantities and their relationships in problem situations. They bring two complementary abilities to bear on problems involving quantitative relationships: the ability to decontextualize—to abstract a given situation and represent it symbolically and manipulate the representing symbols as if they have a life of their own, without necessarily attending to their referents—and the ability to contextualize, to pause as needed during the manipulation process in order to probe into the referents for the symbols involved. Quantitative reasoning entails habits of creating a coherent representation of the problem at hand; considering the units involved; attending to the meaning of quantities, not just how to compute them; and knowing and flexibly using different properties of operations and objects.

data.title: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others
data.description: Mathematically proficient students understand and use stated assumptions, definitions, and previously established results in constructing arguments. They make conjectures and build a logical progression of statements to explore the truth of their conjectures. They are able to analyze situations by breaking them into cases, and can recognize and use counterexamples. They justify their conclusions, communicate them to others, and respond to the arguments of others. They reason inductively about data, making plausible arguments that take into account the context from which the data arose. Mathematically proficient students are also able to compare the effectiveness of two plausible arguments, distinguish correct logic or reasoning from that which is flawed, and—if there is a flaw in an argument—explain what it is. Elementary students can construct arguments using concrete referents such as objects, drawings, diagrams, and actions. Such arguments can make sense and be correct, even though they are not generalized or made formal until later grades. Later, students learn to determine domains to which an argument applies. Students at all grades can listen or read the arguments of others, decide whether they make sense, and ask useful questions to clarify or improve the arguments.

data.title: Model with mathematics
data.description: Mathematically proficient students can apply the mathematics they know to solve problems arising in everyday life, society, and the workplace. In early grades, this might be as simple as writing an addition equation to describe a situation. In middle grades, a student might apply proportional reasoning to plan a school event or analyze a problem in the community. By high school, a student might use geometry to solve a design problem or use a function to describe how one quantity of interest depends on another. Mathematically proficient students who can apply what they know are comfortable making assumptions and approximations to simplify a complicated situation, realizing that these may need revision later. They are able to identify important quantities in a practical situation and map their relationships using such tools as diagrams, two-way tables, graphs, flowcharts and formulas. They can analyze those relationships mathematically to draw conclusions. They routinely interpret their mathematical results in the context of the situation and reflect on whether the results make sense, possibly improving the model if it has not served its purpose.

data.title: Use appropriate tools strategically
data.description: Mathematically proficient students consider the available tools when solving a mathematical problem. These tools might include pencil and paper, concrete models, a ruler, a protractor, a calculator, a spreadsheet, a computer algebra system, a statistical package, or dynamic geometry software. Proficient students are sufficiently familiar with tools appropriate for their grade or course to make sound decisions about when each of these tools might be helpful, recognizing both the insight to be gained and their limitations. For example, mathematically proficient high school students analyze graphs of functions and solutions generated using a graphing calculator. They detect possible errors by strategically using estimation and other mathematical knowledge. When making mathematical models, they know that technology can enable them to visualize the results of varying assumptions, explore consequences, and compare predictions with data. Mathematically proficient students at various grade levels are able to identify relevant external mathematical resources, such as digital content located on a website, and use them to pose or solve problems. They are able to use technological tools to explore and deepen their understanding of concepts.

data.title: Attend to precision
data.description: Mathematically proficient students try to communicate precisely to others. They try to use clear definitions in discussion with others and in their own reasoning. They state the meaning of the symbols they choose, including using the equal sign consistently and appropriately. They are careful about specifying units of measure, and labeling axes to clarify the correspondence with quantities in a problem. They calculate accurately and efficiently, express numerical answers with a degree of precision appropriate for the problem context. In the elementary grades, students give carefully formulated explanations to each other. By the time they reach high school they have learned to examine claims and make explicit use of definitions.

data.title: Look for and make use of structure
data.description: Mathematically proficient students look closely to discern a pattern or structure. Young students, for example, might notice that three and seven more is the same amount as seven and three more, or they may sort a collection of shapes according to how many sides the shapes have. Later, students will see 7 × 8 equals the well remembered 7 × 5 + 7 × 3, in preparation for learning about the distributive property. In the expression x² + 9x + 14, older students can see the 14 as 2 × 7 and the 9 as 2 + 7. They recognize the significance of an existing line in a geometric figure and can use the strategy of drawing an auxiliary line for solving problems. They also can step back for an overview and shift perspective. They can see complicated things, such as some algebraic expressions, as single objects or as being composed of several objects. For example, they can see 5 – 3(x – y)² as 5 minus a positive number times a square and use that to realize that its value cannot be more than 5 for any real numbers x and y.

data.title: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning
data.description: Mathematically proficient students notice if calculations are repeated, and look both for general methods and for shortcuts. Upper elementary students might notice when dividing 25 by 11 that they are repeating the same calculations over and over again, and conclude they have a repeating decimal. By paying attention to the calculation of slope as they repeatedly check whether points are on the line through (1, 2) with slope 3, middle school students might abstract the equation (y – 2)/(x – 1) = 3. Noticing the regularity in the way terms cancel when expanding (x – 1)(x + 1), (x – 1)(x² + x + 1), and (x – 1)(x³ + x² + x + 1) might lead them to the general formula for the sum of a geometric series. As they work to solve a problem, mathematically proficient students maintain oversight of the process, while attending to the details. They continually evaluate the reasonableness of their intermediate results.

data.title: N-CN.1
data.cluster: Perform arithmetic operations with complex numbers.
data.description: Know there is a complex number i such that i^2 = –1, and every complex number has the form a + bi with a and b real.

data.title: N-CN.2
data.cluster: Perform arithmetic operations with complex numbers.
data.description: Use the relation i^2 = –1 and the commutative, associative, and distributive properties to add, subtract, and multiply complex numbers.

data.title: N-CN.3
data.cluster: Perform arithmetic operations with complex numbers.
data.description: (+) Find the conjugate of a complex number; use conjugates to find moduli and quotients of complex numbers.

data.title: N-CN.4
data.cluster: Represent complex numbers and their operations on the complex plane.
data.description: (+) Represent complex numbers on the complex plane in rectangular and polar form (including real and imaginary numbers), and explain why the rectangular and polar forms of a given complex number represent the same number.

data.title: N-CN.5
data.cluster: Represent complex numbers and their operations on the complex plane.
data.description: (+) Represent addition, subtraction, multiplication, and conjugation of complex numbers geometrically on the complex plane; use properties of this representation for computation. For example, (–1 + √3 i)^3 = 8 because (–1 + √3 i) has modulus 2 and argument 120°.

data.title: N-CN.6
data.cluster: Represent complex numbers and their operations on the complex plane.
data.description: (+) Calculate the distance between numbers in the complex plane as the modulus of the difference, and the midpoint of a segment as the average of the numbers at its endpoints.

data.title: N-CN.7
data.cluster: Use complex numbers in polynomial identities and equations.
data.description: Solve quadratic equations with real coefficients that have complex solutions.

data.title: N-CN.8
data.cluster: Use complex numbers in polynomial identities and equations.
data.description: (+) Extend polynomial identities to the complex numbers. For example, rewrite x^2 + 4 as (x + 2i)(x – 2i).

data.title: N-CN.9
data.cluster: Use complex numbers in polynomial identities and equations.
data.description: (+) Know the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra; show that it is true for quadratic polynomials.

data.title: N-Q.1
data.cluster: Reason quantitatively and use units to solve problems.
data.description: Use units as a way to understand problems and to guide the solution of multi-step problems; choose and interpret units consistently in formulas; choose and interpret the scale and the origin in graphs and data displays.

data.title: N-Q.2
data.cluster: Reason quantitatively and use units to solve problems.
data.description: Define appropriate quantities for the purpose of descriptive modeling.

data.title: N-Q.3
data.cluster: Reason quantitatively and use units to solve problems.
data.description: Choose a level of accuracy appropriate to limitations on measurement when reporting quantities.

data.title: N-RN.1
data.cluster: Extend the properties of exponents to rational exponents.
data.description: Explain how the definition of the meaning of rational exponents follows from extending the properties of integer exponents to those values, allowing for a notation for radicals in terms of rational exponents. For example, we define 5^1/3 to be the cube root of 5 because we want (5^1/3)^3 = 5^(1/3)3 to hold, so (5^1/3)^3 must equal 5.

data.title: N-RN.2
data.cluster: Extend the properties of exponents to rational exponents.
data.description: Rewrite expressions involving radicals and rational exponents using the properties of exponents.

data.title: N-RN.3
data.cluster: Use properties of rational and irrational numbers.
data.description: Explain why the sum or product of two rational numbers is rational; that the sum of a rational number and an irrational number is irrational; and that the product of a nonzero rational number and an irrational number is irrational.

data.title: N-VM.1
data.cluster: Represent and model with vector quantities.
data.description: (+) Recognize vector quantities as having both magnitude and direction. Represent vector quantities by directed line segments, and use appropriate symbols for vectors and their magnitudes (e.g., v, |v|, ||v||, v).

data.title: N-VM.10
data.cluster: Perform operations on matrices and use matrices in applications.
data.description: (+) Understand that the zero and identity matrices play a role in matrix addition and multiplication similar to the role of 0 and 1 in the real numbers. The determinant of a square matrix is nonzero if and only if the matrix has a multiplicative inverse.

data.title: N-VM.11
data.cluster: Perform operations on matrices and use matrices in applications.
data.description: (+) Multiply a vector (regarded as a matrix with one column) by a matrix of suitable dimensions to produce another vector. Work with matrices as transformations of vectors.

data.title: N-VM.12
data.cluster: Perform operations on matrices and use matrices in applications.
data.description: (+) Work with 2 × 2 matrices as transformations of the plane, and interpret the absolute value of the determinant in terms of area.

data.title: N-VM.2
data.cluster: Represent and model with vector quantities.
data.description: (+) Find the components of a vector by subtracting the coordinates of an initial point from the coordinates of a terminal point.

data.title: N-VM.3
data.cluster: Represent and model with vector quantities.
data.description: (+) Solve problems involving velocity and other quantities that can be represented by vectors.

data.title: N-VM.4a
data.description: Add vectors end-to-end, component-wise, and by the parallelogram rule. Understand that the magnitude of a sum of two vectors is typically not the sum of the magnitudes.

data.title: N-VM.4b
data.description: Given two vectors in magnitude and direction form, determine the magnitude and direction of their sum.

data.title: N-VM.4c
data.description: Understand vector subtraction v – w as v + (–w), where –w is the additive inverse of w, with the same magnitude as w and pointing in the opposite direction. Represent vector subtraction graphically by connecting the tips in the appropriate order, and perform vector subtraction component-wise.

data.title: N-VM.5a
data.description: Represent scalar multiplication graphically by scaling vectors and possibly reversing their direction; perform scalar multiplication component-wise, e.g., as c(vx, vy) = (cvx, cvy).

data.title: N-VM.5b
data.description: Compute the magnitude of a scalar multiple cv using ||cv|| = |c|v. Compute the direction of cv knowing that when |c|v ≠ 0, the direction of cv is either along v (for c > 0) or against v (for c < 0).

data.title: N-VM.6
data.cluster: Perform operations on matrices and use matrices in applications.
data.description: (+) Use matrices to represent and manipulate data, e.g., to represent payoffs or incidence relationships in a network.

data.title: N-VM.7
data.cluster: Perform operations on matrices and use matrices in applications.
data.description: (+) Multiply matrices by scalars to produce new matrices, e.g., as when all of the payoffs in a game are doubled.

data.title: N-VM.8
data.cluster: Perform operations on matrices and use matrices in applications.
data.description: (+) Add, subtract, and multiply matrices of appropriate dimensions.

data.title: N-VM.9
data.cluster: Perform operations on matrices and use matrices in applications.
data.description: (+) Understand that, unlike multiplication of numbers, matrix multiplication for square matrices is not a commutative operation, but still satisfies the associative and distributive properties.

data.title: Describe events as subsets of a sample space
data.domain: Conditional Probability and the Rules of Probability
data.cluster: Understand independence and conditional probability and use them to interpret data
data.description: Describe events as subsets of a sample space (the set of outcomes) using characteristics (or categories) of the outcomes, or as unions, intersections, or complements of other events (“or,” “and,” “not”).

data.title: Understand independent events
data.domain: Conditional Probability and the Rules of Probability
data.cluster: Understand independence and conditional probability and use them to interpret data
data.description: Understand that two events A and B are independent if the probability of A and B occurring together is the product of their probabilities, and use this characterization to determine if they are independent.

data.title: Understand conditional probability and independence
data.domain: Conditional Probability and the Rules of Probability
data.cluster: Understand independence and conditional probability and use them to interpret data
data.description: Understand the conditional probability of A given B as P(A and B)/P(B), and interpret independence of A and B as saying that the conditional probability of A given B is the same as the probability of A, and the conditional probability of B given A is the same as the probability of B.

data.title: Construct and interpret two-way frequency tables
data.domain: Conditional Probability and the Rules of Probability
data.cluster: Understand independence and conditional probability and use them to interpret data
data.description: Construct and interpret two-way frequency tables of data when two categories are associated with each object being classified. Use the two-way table as a sample space to decide if events are independent and to approximate conditional probabilities. For example, collect data from a random sample of students in your school on their favorite subject among math, science, and English. Estimate the probability that a randomly selected student from your school will favor science given that the student is in tenth grade. Do the same for other subjects and compare the results.

data.title: Recognize conditional probability and independence in everyday situations
data.domain: Conditional Probability and the Rules of Probability
data.cluster: Understand independence and conditional probability and use them to interpret data
data.description: Recognize and explain the concepts of conditional probability and independence in everyday language and everyday situations. For example, compare the chance of having lung cancer if you are a smoker with the chance of being a smoker if you have lung cancer.

data.title: Find conditional probability as a fraction of outcomes
data.domain: Conditional Probability and the Rules of Probability
data.cluster: Use the rules of probability to compute probabilities of compound events in a uniform probability model
data.description: Find the conditional probability of A given B as the fraction of B’s outcomes that also belong to A, and interpret the answer in terms of the model.

data.title: Apply the Addition Rule
data.domain: Conditional Probability and the Rules of Probability
data.cluster: Use the rules of probability to compute probabilities of compound events in a uniform probability model
data.description: Apply the Addition Rule, P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) – P(A and B), and interpret the answer in terms of the model.

data.title: Apply the general Multiplication Rule
data.domain: Conditional Probability and the Rules of Probability
data.cluster: Use the rules of probability to compute probabilities of compound events in a uniform probability model
data.description: (+) Apply the general Multiplication Rule in a uniform probability model, P(A and B) = P(A)P(B|A) = P(B)P(A|B), and interpret the answer in terms of the model.

data.title: Use permutations and combinations
data.domain: Conditional Probability and the Rules of Probability
data.cluster: Use the rules of probability to compute probabilities of compound events in a uniform probability model
data.description: (+) Use permutations and combinations to compute probabilities of compound events and solve problems.

data.title: Understand statistics as a process for making inferences
data.cluster: Understand and evaluate random processes underlying statistical experiments
data.description: Understand statistics as a process for making inferences about population parameters based on a random sample from that population.

data.title: Decide if a model is consistent with results
data.cluster: Understand and evaluate random processes underlying statistical experiments
data.description: Decide if a specified model is consistent with results from a given data-generating process, e.g., using simulation. For example, a model says a spinning coin falls heads up with probability 0.5. Would a result of 5 tails in a row cause you to question the model?

data.title: Recognize purposes of surveys, experiments, and observational studies
data.cluster: Make inferences and justify conclusions from sample surveys, experiments, and observational studies
data.description: Recognize the purposes of and differences among sample surveys, experiments, and observational studies; explain how randomization relates to each.

data.title: Use sample survey data to estimate population mean or proportion
data.cluster: Make inferences and justify conclusions from sample surveys, experiments, and observational studies
data.description: Use data from a sample survey to estimate a population mean or proportion; develop a margin of error through the use of simulation models for random sampling.

data.title: Use randomized experiment data to compare treatments
data.cluster: Make inferences and justify conclusions from sample surveys, experiments, and observational studies
data.description: Use data from a randomized experiment to compare two treatments; use simulations to decide if differences between parameters are significant.

data.title: Evaluate reports based on data
data.cluster: Make inferences and justify conclusions from sample surveys, experiments, and observational studies
data.description: Evaluate reports based on data.

data.title: Represent data with plots
data.cluster: Summarize, represent, and interpret data on a single count or measurement variable
data.description: Represent data with plots on the real number line (dot plots, histograms, and box plots).

data.title: Compare center and spread of data distributions
data.cluster: Summarize, represent, and interpret data on a single count or measurement variable
data.description: Use statistics appropriate to the shape of the data distribution to compare center (median, mean) and spread (interquartile range, standard deviation) of two or more different data sets.

data.title: Interpret differences in shape, center, and spread
data.cluster: Summarize, represent, and interpret data on a single count or measurement variable
data.description: Interpret differences in shape, center, and spread in the context of the data sets, accounting for possible effects of extreme data points (outliers).

data.title: Use mean and standard deviation for normal distributions
data.cluster: Summarize, represent, and interpret data on a single count or measurement variable
data.description: Use the mean and standard deviation of a data set to fit it to a normal distribution and to estimate population percentages. Recognize that there are data sets for which such a procedure is not appropriate. Use calculators, spreadsheets, and tables to estimate areas under the normal curve.

data.title: Summarize categorical data in two-way frequency tables
data.cluster: Summarize, represent, and interpret data on two categorical and quantitative variables
data.description: Summarize categorical data for two categories in two-way frequency tables. Interpret relative frequencies in the context of the data (including joint, marginal, and conditional relative frequencies). Recognize possible associations and trends in the data.

data.title: Represent data on two quantitative variables on a scatter plot
data.cluster: Summarize, represent, and interpret data on two categorical and quantitative variables
data.description: Represent data on two quantitative variables on a scatter plot, and describe how the variables are related.

data.title: Fit a function to data
data.cluster: Summarize, represent, and interpret data on two categorical and quantitative variables
data.description: Fit a function to the data; use functions fitted to data to solve problems in the context of the data. Use given functions or choose a function suggested by the context. Emphasize linear, quadratic, and exponential models.

data.title: Assess fit of a function using residuals
data.cluster: Summarize, represent, and interpret data on two categorical and quantitative variables
data.description: Informally assess the fit of a function by plotting and analyzing residuals.

data.title: Fit a linear function for linear associations
data.cluster: Summarize, represent, and interpret data on two categorical and quantitative variables
data.description: Fit a linear function for a scatter plot that suggests a linear association.

data.title: Interpret slope and intercept of a linear model
data.description: Interpret the slope (rate of change) and the intercept (constant term) of a linear model in the context of the data.

data.title: Compute and interpret correlation coefficient
data.description: Compute (using technology) and interpret the correlation coefficient of a linear fit.

data.title: Distinguish between correlation and causation
data.description: Distinguish between correlation and causation.

data.title: Define a random variable
data.cluster: Calculate expected values and use them to solve problems
data.description: (+) Define a random variable for a quantity of interest by assigning a numerical value to each event in a sample space; graph the corresponding probability distribution using the same graphical displays as for data distributions.

data.title: Calculate expected value of a random variable
data.cluster: Calculate expected values and use them to solve problems
data.description: (+) Calculate the expected value of a random variable; interpret it as the mean of the probability distribution.

data.title: Develop a probability distribution (theoretical)
data.cluster: Calculate expected values and use them to solve problems
data.description: (+) Develop a probability distribution for a random variable defined for a sample space in which theoretical probabilities can be calculated; find the expected value. For example, find the theoretical probability distribution for the number of correct answers obtained by guessing on all five questions of a multiple-choice test where each question has four choices, and find the expected grade under various grading schemes.

data.title: Develop a probability distribution (empirical)
data.cluster: Calculate expected values and use them to solve problems
data.description: (+) Develop a probability distribution for a random variable defined for a sample space in which probabilities are assigned empirically; find the expected value. For example, find a current data distribution on the number of TV sets per household in the United States, and calculate the expected number of sets per household. How many TV sets would you expect to find in 100 randomly selected households?

data.title: Weigh possible outcomes of a decision
data.cluster: Use probability to evaluate outcomes of decisions
data.description: (+) Weigh the possible outcomes of a decision by assigning probabilities to payoff values and finding expected values.

data.title: Find expected payoff
data.cluster: Use probability to evaluate outcomes of decisions
data.description: Find the expected payoff for a game of chance. For example, find the expected winnings from a state lottery ticket or a game at a fast-food restaurant.

data.title: Evaluate and compare strategies
data.cluster: Use probability to evaluate outcomes of decisions
data.description: Evaluate and compare strategies on the basis of expected values. For example, compare a high-deductible versus a low-deductible automobile insurance policy using various, but reasonable, chances of having a minor or a major accident.

data.title: Use probabilities to make fair decisions
data.cluster: Use probability to evaluate outcomes of decisions
data.description: (+) Use probabilities to make fair decisions (e.g., drawing by lots, using a random number generator).

data.title: Analyze decisions and strategies
data.cluster: Use probability to evaluate outcomes of decisions
data.description: (+) Analyze decisions and strategies using probability concepts (e.g., product testing, medical testing, pulling a hockey goalie at the end of a game).
